faq.q: What are the QLD transfer duty rates?
faq.a: Queensland charges transfer duty on a sliding scale: 1.5% up to $75,000, 3.5% on $75,001-$540,000, 4.5% on $540,001-$1,000,000, and 5.75% on amounts over $1,000,000.

faq.q: What is the QLD first home concession?
faq.a: Queensland first home buyers purchasing an existing or new home valued up to $700,000 pay no transfer duty. A sliding concession applies for homes valued $700,001-$800,000. The property must be your principal place of residence and you must move in within 1 year.

faq.q: What is the Queensland First Home Owner Grant?
faq.a: QLD offers a $30,000 First Home Owner Grant for new homes valued up to $750,000 (effective from 20 November 2023). This is one of the most generous FHOG in Australia and applies to new builds and new homes only, not existing properties.

faq.q: What is the AFAD surcharge in QLD?
faq.a: The Additional Foreign Acquirer Duty (AFAD) in Queensland is 8% of the property's dutiable value, charged on top of standard transfer duty for foreign buyers of residential property.

howItWorks: The calculator applies Queensland's Office of State Revenue transfer duty rates using their sliding-scale brackets. First home buyer concessions reduce or eliminate duty for eligible properties up to the threshold. The AFAD surcharge of 8% is added for foreign purchasers. The result shows total duty payable and the effective rate.

title: Stamp Duty Calculator — All States
description: Compare stamp duty across all Australian states and territories on one page.
metaDescription: Compare stamp duty across all Australian states and territories. See NSW, VIC, QLD, WA, SA, TAS, ACT and NT transfer duty side by side.

faq.q: Which Australian state has the lowest stamp duty?
faq.a: The Northern Territory generally has the lowest stamp duty rates. The ACT has been phasing out stamp duty in favour of an annual land tax since 2012, so new purchasers there may pay an annual charge instead. Queensland and Tasmania also tend to be lower than NSW and VIC for mid-range properties.

faq.q: Which state has the best first home buyer stamp duty deal?
faq.a: Queensland is arguably the most generous — no duty on homes up to $700,000 plus a $30,000 FHOG for new builds. NSW offers no duty up to $800,000. VIC exempts up to $600,000. All states have some form of FHB concession.

faq.q: Do all states charge foreign buyer surcharges?
faq.a: All states and territories except the Northern Territory charge an additional foreign buyer surcharge on residential property, ranging from 7% to 8% depending on the jurisdiction.

faq.q: Is stamp duty a one-off cost?
faq.a: Yes. Transfer duty (stamp duty) is a one-off cost paid at settlement when you purchase a property. It is not recurring. The ACT is the exception — they offer an option to pay an annual property tax instead of upfront duty.

howItWorks: The calculator applies each state's transfer duty schedule to the property value. When 'Compare All States' is selected, it shows duty payable side by side for all eight jurisdictions at the same property value. First home buyer concessions and foreign buyer surcharges are applied according to each state's specific rules.

title: Land Tax Calculator
description: Calculate annual land tax by state based on the unimproved value of your land.
metaDescription: Calculate Australian land tax by state. Enter your land value and see annual land tax for NSW, VIC, QLD, WA, SA, TAS, ACT or NT.

inputs.label: Unimproved Land Value
inputs.help: As shown on your council rates notice or land valuation

inputs.label: Is This Your Principal Residence?
inputs.help: Your main home is generally exempt from land tax in all states

inputs.label: Ownership Entity
inputs.help: Trusts may attract surcharge rates in some states

faq.q: Is my primary residence subject to land tax?
faq.a: No. In all Australian states and territories, your principal place of residence is exempt from land tax. Land tax applies to investment properties, vacant land, commercial properties, and holiday homes.

faq.q: How is land value determined?
faq.a: Each state's Valuer General assesses the unimproved value of land (the value of the land without any buildings or improvements). This valuation is updated periodically and appears on your council rates notice.

faq.q: Do trusts pay higher land tax?
faq.a: In some states, yes. NSW charges a trust surcharge of 0.25% on land held in trusts. Victoria charges a trust surcharge of 0.375%. These are on top of the standard land tax rates. Some states don't differentiate.

faq.q: What is the land tax threshold?
faq.a: Each state has a threshold below which no land tax is payable. For example, NSW starts at $1,075,000 (2025), VIC at $50,000 (general) or $300,000 (primary production), and QLD at $600,000. The threshold applies to total taxable land holdings, not individual properties.

howItWorks: The calculator applies the selected state's land tax rates to the unimproved land value. It checks whether the value exceeds the tax-free threshold for that state and applies the appropriate marginal rates. Trust surcharges are added if applicable. Primary residence is shown as exempt. The result shows annual land tax payable.

title: First Home Buyer Cost Calculator
description: Calculate the total upfront costs of buying your first home in Australia, including deposit, stamp duty, and LMI.
metaDescription: Calculate all upfront costs of buying your first home in Australia. Deposit, stamp duty, LMI, conveyancing, inspections and more.

inputs.label: Deposit Percentage
inputs.help: Deposits below 20% typically require LMI

faq.q: What upfront costs do first home buyers face in Australia?
faq.a: Key costs include: deposit (5-20%), stamp duty (varies by state, often reduced for FHBs), Lenders Mortgage Insurance (if deposit < 20%), conveyancing ($1,500-$3,000), building and pest inspections ($500-$1,000), loan application fees ($0-$600), title search ($20-$100), and moving costs ($500-$2,000).

faq.q: Can I buy a home with less than 20% deposit?
faq.a: Yes. Many lenders accept 5-10% deposits, but you'll pay Lenders Mortgage Insurance (LMI) which can be $5,000-$30,000+. The government's Home Guarantee Scheme allows eligible buyers to purchase with as little as 5% deposit without paying LMI.

faq.q: What government grants are available for first home buyers?
faq.a: Grants vary by state: QLD offers $30,000 FHOG for new homes up to $750,000. NSW offers $10,000 for new homes up to $600,000. VIC offers $10,000 for new homes up to $750,000. All states offer stamp duty concessions. The federal Home Guarantee Scheme covers LMI for eligible buyers.

faq.q: What is the First Home Super Saver Scheme?
faq.a: The FHSSS lets you withdraw voluntary super contributions (up to $50,000) to use as a home deposit. You save on tax because salary-sacrificed contributions are taxed at 15% instead of your marginal rate. You apply through the ATO when ready to purchase.

howItWorks: The calculator adds up all upfront costs: deposit amount (based on your percentage), stamp duty (using the selected state's rates with FHB concessions), LMI estimate (if LVR > 80%), conveyancing fees, inspection costs, and miscellaneous charges. It subtracts any applicable First Home Owner Grant to show your total cash needed at settlement.

title: LVR Calculator
description: Calculate your Loan-to-Value Ratio and whether you'll need Lenders Mortgage Insurance.
metaDescription: Calculate your Loan-to-Value Ratio (LVR) for an Australian property purchase. See if you need Lenders Mortgage Insurance.

inputs.label: Loan Amount
inputs.help: The amount you need to borrow

faq.q: What is LVR?
faq.a: Loan-to-Value Ratio (LVR) is the percentage of a property's value that you're borrowing. LVR = (Loan Amount / Property Value) x 100. An LVR of 80% means you're borrowing 80% and have a 20% deposit or equity.

faq.q: What LVR triggers LMI?
faq.a: Most Australian lenders require Lenders Mortgage Insurance (LMI) when LVR exceeds 80%. Some lenders offer LMI-free loans up to 85% LVR for certain professionals (doctors, lawyers, accountants). Above 90% LVR, LMI premiums increase significantly.

faq.q: Does LVR affect my interest rate?
faq.a: Yes. Many lenders offer tiered interest rates based on LVR. You'll typically get the best rates at LVR of 60% or below. Rates may increase at LVR brackets of 70%, 80%, and 90%.

faq.q: How can I reduce my LVR?
faq.a: Save a larger deposit, use a guarantor (family guarantee lets you borrow 100%+ LVR without LMI), buy a cheaper property, or use the First Home Guarantee Scheme to avoid LMI with just 5% deposit.

howItWorks: The calculator divides the loan amount by the property value and multiplies by 100 to get the LVR percentage. It then classifies the result into risk bands: low risk (< 60%), standard (60-80%), LMI zone (80-90%), and high LMI (90%+). It shows the deposit amount implied by the LVR and flags whether LMI is likely required.

title: LMI Calculator
description: Estimate your Lenders Mortgage Insurance premium based on property value and loan-to-value ratio.
metaDescription: Estimate Lenders Mortgage Insurance (LMI) costs for your Australian home loan. See how deposit size affects your LMI premium.

inputs.label: First Home Buyer?
inputs.help: Some lenders offer LMI discounts for FHBs

inputs.label: State
inputs.help: Stamp duty on LMI varies by state

faq.q: What is Lenders Mortgage Insurance?
faq.a: LMI is insurance that protects the lender (not you) if you default on your home loan. It's required when you borrow more than 80% of the property value (LVR > 80%). The premium is a one-off cost paid at settlement, though it can be capitalised into the loan.

faq.q: How much does LMI cost?
faq.a: LMI premiums depend on loan amount and LVR. Rough guide: at 85% LVR on a $700,000 property, expect $5,000-$8,000. At 90% LVR, $12,000-$18,000. At 95% LVR, $25,000-$35,000. Premiums increase steeply as LVR rises above 90%.

faq.q: Can I avoid paying LMI?
faq.a: Yes. Save a 20%+ deposit, use a family guarantee where a parent's property secures the shortfall, qualify for the government Home Guarantee Scheme (5% deposit, no LMI), or be in an exempt profession (some lenders waive LMI for doctors, lawyers at up to 90% LVR).

faq.q: Is LMI refundable?
faq.a: Partially. If you pay off or refinance your loan within the first few years, you may be eligible for a partial LMI refund. Refund policies vary by insurer — most offer sliding-scale refunds decreasing over the first 2-3 years.

howItWorks: The calculator computes LVR from your loan amount and property value, then estimates the LMI premium using published premium tables from major LMI providers (Helia and QBE). Premiums are based on LVR bands and loan amount tiers. Stamp duty on the LMI premium (which varies by state) is added. The result shows the estimated LMI premium and total upfront cost.

title: BMI Calculator
description: Calculate your Body Mass Index and see which weight category you fall into.
metaDescription: Calculate your BMI with this free Australian BMI calculator. See your weight category and healthy weight range for your height.

faq.q: What are the BMI categories?
faq.a: WHO categories: Underweight < 18.5, Normal weight 18.5-24.9, Overweight 25.0-29.9, Obese class I 30.0-34.9, Obese class II 35.0-39.9, Obese class III 40.0+. For people of Asian background, overweight begins at 23.0 and obese at 27.5.

faq.q: Is BMI accurate for everyone?
faq.a: BMI has limitations. It doesn't distinguish between muscle and fat, so athletes may be classified as overweight. It may also underestimate body fat in older adults who have lost muscle mass. Waist circumference is a useful additional measure.

faq.q: What is a healthy weight for an Australian adult?
faq.a: According to the Australian Institute of Health and Welfare, a healthy BMI is 18.5-24.9. In 2022, about 67% of Australian adults were overweight or obese. Maintaining a healthy weight reduces risk of type 2 diabetes, heart disease, and certain cancers.

howItWorks: BMI is calculated as weight (kg) divided by height (m) squared: BMI = weight / height². The calculator then classifies your result into standard WHO categories and shows the healthy weight range for your height (BMI 18.5-24.9). It also estimates the weight you'd need to reach to move to a lower category.

title: Daily Energy (TDEE) Calculator
description: Calculate your Total Daily Energy Expenditure and Basal Metabolic Rate based on activity level.
metaDescription: Calculate your TDEE and BMR. Find out how many calories you burn per day based on your age, gender, height, weight and activity level.

faq.q: What is TDEE?
faq.a: Total Daily Energy Expenditure is the total number of calories you burn in a day, including your Basal Metabolic Rate (calories at complete rest), thermic effect of food (digestion), and physical activity. TDEE determines how many calories you need to eat to maintain your current weight.

faq.q: What is the difference between BMR and TDEE?
faq.a: BMR (Basal Metabolic Rate) is the calories your body burns just to keep you alive at rest — breathing, circulation, cell production. TDEE is BMR multiplied by an activity factor. For a moderately active person, TDEE is roughly 1.55x their BMR.

faq.q: Which BMR formula is most accurate?
faq.a: The Mifflin-St Jeor equation is considered the most accurate for most people and is recommended by the Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics. It was developed in 1990 and tends to estimate BMR within 10% of measured values.

faq.q: How many calories does the average Australian need?
faq.a: General guidelines suggest around 2,000-2,200 kcal/day for adult women and 2,400-2,800 kcal/day for adult men, depending on age and activity level. Individual needs vary significantly — use your TDEE as a personalised starting point.

howItWorks: The calculator computes your BMR using the Mifflin-St Jeor equation: Men = 10 x weight(kg) + 6.25 x height(cm) - 5 x age - 161 + 166. It then multiplies BMR by an activity factor (1.2 for sedentary up to 1.9 for extremely active) to estimate your TDEE. Both BMR and TDEE are shown in calories (kcal) and kilojoules (kJ).

title: Calorie Intake Calculator
description: Calculate your daily calorie and macronutrient targets for weight loss, maintenance, or muscle gain.
metaDescription: Calculate your daily calorie intake for weight loss, maintenance or gain. Includes macronutrient breakdown for protein, carbs and fat.

inputs.label: Macro Split
inputs.help: Protein / Carbs / Fat percentages

faq.q: How many calories should I eat to lose weight?
faq.a: A safe calorie deficit is 500 kcal/day below your TDEE, which produces roughly 0.5 kg weight loss per week. A 1,000 kcal/day deficit gives ~1 kg/week loss but is harder to sustain. Women should generally not go below 1,200 kcal/day and men 1,500 kcal/day without medical supervision.

faq.q: How much protein do I need?
faq.a: The Australian RDI is 0.84g/kg for men and 0.75g/kg for women. For active people or those trying to lose weight while preserving muscle, 1.6-2.2g/kg of body weight is commonly recommended by sports dietitians.

faq.q: What is a macro split?
faq.a: A macro split is the percentage of your daily calories coming from protein, carbohydrates, and fat. A balanced split (30/35/35) suits most people. Higher protein ratios help with muscle retention during weight loss. Low-carb and keto reduce carbohydrates and increase fat intake.

howItWorks: The calculator first computes your TDEE using the Mifflin-St Jeor BMR formula and activity multiplier. It then adjusts calories based on your goal: -500 kcal/day for moderate loss, -1000 for fast loss, +500 for gain, or no change for maintenance. Finally, it splits total calories into grams of protein, carbs, and fat using your selected macro ratio (1g protein = 4 kcal, 1g carbs = 4 kcal, 1g fat = 9 kcal).

title: Pregnancy Due Date Calculator
description: Calculate your estimated due date, current week, and trimester from your last menstrual period or conception date.
metaDescription: Calculate your pregnancy due date based on your last period or conception date. See your current week, trimester and key milestones.

inputs.label: Date
inputs.help: Select the relevant date based on your chosen method

inputs.label: Average Cycle Length
inputs.help: Only used for LMP method — standard is 28 days

faq.q: How is the due date calculated?
faq.a: Using Naegele's Rule: add 280 days (40 weeks) from the first day of your last menstrual period (LMP). If your cycle is longer or shorter than 28 days, the calculation adjusts by the difference. Only about 5% of babies arrive on their exact due date — most arrive within 2 weeks either side.

faq.q: When are the trimesters?
faq.a: First trimester: weeks 1-12. Second trimester: weeks 13-27. Third trimester: weeks 28-40. Key milestones include the first ultrasound around 12 weeks, anatomy scan at 18-20 weeks, and viability at 24 weeks.

faq.q: When should I have my first prenatal appointment in Australia?
faq.a: Book your first appointment with a GP or midwife as soon as your pregnancy is confirmed, ideally before 10 weeks. Your first hospital booking appointment is usually around 12-16 weeks. Medicare covers most prenatal care in the public system.

faq.q: What are the key dates in an Australian pregnancy?
faq.a: 12-week scan (nuchal translucency), 18-20 week anatomy scan, 24-28 week glucose tolerance test, 36-week Group B Strep test, and weekly appointments from 36 weeks. Paid Parental Leave (government) requires lodging a claim with Services Australia before the birth.

howItWorks: For the LMP method, the calculator adds 280 days to your last period date, adjusting for cycle lengths other than 28 days. For conception date, it adds 266 days. For IVF, it adds 266 days minus the embryo age (3 or 5 days). It shows your estimated due date, current gestational week, trimester, and days remaining.

title: Age Calculator
description: Calculate your exact age in years, months, and days — plus your next birthday and the day you were born.
metaDescription: Calculate your exact age in years, months and days from your date of birth. Find out your next birthday and what day you were born on.

inputs.label: Date of Birth
inputs.help: Enter your date of birth

inputs.label: Calculate Age On
inputs.help: Leave blank for today's date, or pick a future/past date

faq.q: How is age calculated exactly?
faq.a: Age in complete years is determined by counting how many full birthdays have passed since your date of birth. Months and days are then counted from your last birthday. This calculator handles leap years correctly.

faq.q: What day of the week was I born on?
faq.a: The calculator shows the day of the week for your date of birth — Monday through Sunday. It uses the standard calendar algorithm to determine this for any date in history.

faq.q: When is my next birthday?
faq.a: The calculator shows the date of your next birthday, how many days until it arrives, and which day of the week it falls on. Handy for planning.

howItWorks: The calculator computes the difference between your date of birth and today (or your chosen target date) in complete years, months, and remaining days. It also shows your age in total days, weeks, and hours. The day of the week for your birth date is computed using a calendar algorithm. Your next birthday date and countdown are derived from the current year.

title: Body Fat Percentage Calculator
description: Estimate your body fat percentage using the US Navy method with simple body measurements.
metaDescription: Calculate your body fat percentage using simple body measurements. Uses the US Navy formula with Australian health guidelines.

inputs.label: Neck Circumference
inputs.help: Measure around the narrowest point below your Adam's apple

inputs.label: Waist Circumference
inputs.help: Measure at your navel, relaxed

inputs.label: Hip Circumference
inputs.help: Women only — measure at the widest point of hips

faq.q: What is a healthy body fat percentage?
faq.a: For men: essential fat 2-5%, athletes 6-13%, fitness 14-17%, acceptable 18-24%, obese 25%+. For women: essential fat 10-13%, athletes 14-20%, fitness 21-24%, acceptable 25-31%, obese 32%+. Women naturally carry more body fat.

faq.q: How accurate is the US Navy method?
faq.a: The US Navy formula is accurate to within about 3-4% of methods like DEXA scanning. It's most accurate for average body types and less reliable for very lean or very muscular individuals. It's a good free estimate — DEXA scans cost $50-$150 in Australia for precise measurement.

faq.q: Is waist circumference important?
faq.a: Yes. The Australian government health guidelines flag increased health risk at waist circumference above 94cm for men and 80cm for women, and substantially increased risk above 102cm for men and 88cm for women, regardless of BMI.

howItWorks: The calculator uses the US Navy body fat formula. For men: 86.010 x log10(waist - neck) - 70.041 x log10(height) + 36.76. For women: 163.205 x log10(waist + hip - neck) - 97.684 x log10(height) - 78.387. All measurements in centimetres. The result is classified into standard body fat categories.

title: Ideal Weight Calculator
description: Calculate your ideal weight range using multiple established formulas based on height, gender, and frame size.
metaDescription: Calculate your ideal weight range using multiple formulas. See Devine, Robinson, Miller and Hamwi ideal body weight for your height.

inputs.label: Frame Size
inputs.help: Wrap thumb and index finger around your wrist: if they overlap you're small, touch is medium, gap is large

faq.q: Which ideal weight formula is most accurate?
faq.a: No single formula is definitively 'best'. The Devine formula (1974) is the most widely used in medicine for drug dosing. Robinson (1983) and Miller (1983) tend to give slightly higher ideal weights. Using the range across all formulas gives a realistic target window.

faq.q: Should I aim for my ideal weight?
faq.a: Ideal weight formulas provide a general guide, not a prescription. Body composition (muscle vs fat), age, and individual health matter more than hitting a specific number. A healthy BMI range (18.5-24.9) for your height is a more practical target.

faq.q: Does frame size matter?
faq.a: Yes. Someone with a larger bone structure and broader shoulders will naturally and healthily weigh more than someone of the same height with a small frame. Frame size adjustments of about 10% up or down from the midpoint are commonly used.

howItWorks: The calculator computes ideal body weight using four established formulas (Devine, Robinson, Miller, Hamwi), each giving a slightly different estimate based on height and gender. Frame size adjustments of +/-10% are applied. The results are shown as a range, along with the BMI-based healthy weight range for comparison.

title: Fuel Cost Calculator
description: Calculate the fuel cost for a trip based on distance, fuel consumption rate, and petrol price.
metaDescription: Calculate fuel cost for any trip in Australia. Enter distance, consumption rate and current petrol price to see trip cost and cost per km.

inputs.label: Fuel Consumption
inputs.help: Check your car's fuel economy sticker or owner's manual

inputs.label: Fuel Price
inputs.help: Current price per litre of your fuel type

faq.q: What is the average fuel consumption of a car in Australia?
faq.a: The average passenger car in Australia uses about 10-11 L/100km. Small cars average 6-8 L/100km, medium sedans 8-10 L/100km, SUVs 9-12 L/100km, and large 4WDs 12-15+ L/100km. EVs use 0 litres but cost about 4-6c per km in electricity.

faq.q: How much does it cost to drive 100km in Australia?
faq.a: At 8.5 L/100km and $1.85/L, driving 100km costs about $15.73 in fuel. Add roughly $5-8 for vehicle running costs (tyres, servicing, depreciation) and your true cost is around $20-24 per 100km.

faq.q: Where can I check current fuel prices?
faq.a: Check FuelWatch (WA), FuelCheck (NSW), or apps like Petrol Spy, GasBuddy, and 7-Eleven's app. Prices vary significantly by suburb — filling up at the bottom of the fuel price cycle can save 20-30c/L.

howItWorks: Fuel cost = (distance / 100) x consumption rate x fuel price. The calculator also shows total litres needed, cost per kilometre, and for return trips doubles the distance. It provides comparison costs for different fuel types (petrol, diesel, LPG, EV) based on typical price ratios.

title: Car Registration Cost Calculator
description: Estimate annual car registration costs by state, including CTP/greenslip where bundled.
metaDescription: Calculate car registration costs in any Australian state. See rego fees, CTP insurance and total annual cost for your vehicle.

inputs.label: Engine Cylinders
inputs.help: Some states base rego on cylinders, weight, or tare mass

inputs.label: Vehicle Tare Weight (kg)
inputs.help: Used in states that charge by weight (VIC, SA)

faq.q: How much does car rego cost in Australia?
faq.a: Typical annual rego for a 4-cylinder car: QLD ~$750-800, NSW ~$700-800 (plus CTP separately), VIC ~$850-950, WA ~$650-750, SA ~$500-600. Costs vary by vehicle size, weight, and whether CTP is bundled (QLD, VIC, ACT, SA, TAS, NT) or separate (NSW, WA).

faq.q: Which state has the cheapest registration?
faq.a: South Australia and the Northern Territory tend to have the lowest overall rego costs. However, you must also factor in CTP/greenslip costs. When CTP is included, QLD and SA generally come out cheapest for standard passenger vehicles.

faq.q: Can I pay rego in instalments?
faq.a: Most states offer 3-month, 6-month, or 12-month registration periods. Shorter periods cost slightly more per month due to admin fees. Some states offer direct debit payment plans. In NSW, you can pay your CTP greenslip separately from rego.

faq.q: Do electric vehicles pay rego in Australia?
faq.a: Yes. EVs pay normal registration fees. Some states previously offered rego discounts for EVs, but most have now introduced or are planning road-user charges (per-km levies) for EVs to replace lost fuel excise revenue.

howItWorks: The calculator looks up base registration fees for the selected state, adjusting for vehicle type, engine size or weight as required by that state's fee schedule. Where CTP is bundled with registration (QLD, VIC, ACT, SA, TAS, NT), an estimated CTP component is included. For NSW and WA where CTP is separate, only the rego component is shown. Fees are shown for your selected period.

title: CTP / Greenslip Estimator
description: Estimate your Compulsory Third Party (CTP) insurance cost based on state, vehicle, and driver details.
metaDescription: Estimate your CTP greenslip insurance cost in any Australian state. Compare third party injury insurance premiums for your vehicle.

inputs.label: Youngest Regular Driver Age
inputs.help: NSW prices vary by age band of the youngest named driver

inputs.label: Postcode
inputs.help: Some states use your postcode to set CTP premium (risk area)

faq.q: What is CTP insurance?
faq.a: Compulsory Third Party (CTP) insurance covers injuries to other people caused by your vehicle. It's mandatory in all states. In NSW it's called a greenslip and purchased from private insurers. In QLD it's part of rego. In VIC it's part of the TAC levy included in rego.

faq.q: How much does CTP cost in NSW?
faq.a: NSW greenslips for a standard passenger car typically range from $400-$700 annually depending on your suburb, driver age, and insurer. Drivers under 25 and in western Sydney postcodes pay more. You can compare greenslip prices at the MAA (SIRA) greenslip comparison tool.

faq.q: Can I choose my CTP insurer?
faq.a: In NSW, you choose from private CTP insurers (AAMI, Allianz, GIO, NRMA, QBE, Youi). In QLD, SA, TAS, ACT, and NT, CTP is provided by a government scheme bundled with rego. In VIC, it's the Transport Accident Commission (TAC). In WA, ICWA is the sole insurer.

faq.q: Is CTP the same as comprehensive car insurance?
faq.a: No. CTP only covers injuries to other people. It does not cover damage to your car, their car, or property. You need separate third-party property and/or comprehensive insurance for those. CTP is compulsory; comprehensive is optional.

howItWorks: The calculator estimates CTP premiums using published rate ranges for each state. In states with private CTP markets (NSW), it uses average premium tables by vehicle type, driver age bracket, and risk postcode. In states where CTP is government-administered, it shows the levy component of registration. This is an estimate — actual premiums vary by insurer.

title: Used Car Value Estimator
description: Estimate the value of a used car based on make, model, year, kilometres, and condition.
metaDescription: Estimate the value of a used car in Australia. Enter make, model, year, kilometres and condition for a price guide.
keywords: how much is my car worth

faq.q: How is a used car valued in Australia?
faq.a: Used car values are determined by make/model, age, odometer reading, condition, service history, and market demand. RedBook and Glass's Guide are the industry standard valuation tools used by dealers and insurers. Private sale prices are typically 10-20% below dealer prices.

faq.q: What is the average kilometres per year for a car?
faq.a: The Australian average is about 12,000-15,000 km per year. A car with significantly lower km than average for its age commands a premium. A 5-year-old car at 50,000 km is below average; at 100,000 km it's above average.

faq.q: Should I sell privately or trade in?
faq.a: Private sale typically gets 10-20% more than trade-in, but involves more effort — advertising, test drives, transfer paperwork. Trade-in is easier and avoids GST implications if you're not registered for it. Carsales, Facebook Marketplace, and Gumtree are the main private sale platforms in Australia.

howItWorks: This estimator uses a baseline value for the make/model/year combination, then adjusts up or down based on kilometres (compared to the expected average for the car's age), condition rating, and transmission type. The result shows an estimated private sale range and dealer retail range. Actual values depend on local market conditions, service history, and features.

title: Toll Calculator
description: Estimate toll costs for common Australian motorway routes and annual commuter costs.
metaDescription: Calculate toll costs for major Australian motorways. See per-trip and annual commuter costs for Sydney, Melbourne and Brisbane toll roads.

inputs.label: Toll Road
inputs.help: Select the toll road or route

inputs.label: Time of Travel
inputs.help: Some tolls vary by time of day

inputs.label: Return Trips Per Week
inputs.help: For calculating annual commuter cost

faq.q: How much do Sydney tolls cost per year?
faq.a: A daily commuter using the M2 and Lane Cove Tunnel pays roughly $15-20 per day in tolls. Over 48 weeks, that's $3,600-$4,800 per year. Some Sydney commuters using multiple toll roads pay over $6,000 annually. The NSW government offers toll relief capping costs at $60/week for frequent users.

faq.q: Is there a toll cap in Sydney?
faq.a: Yes. The NSW Toll Relief program caps weekly toll spending at $60 for private vehicles with an e-tag. If your weekly tolls exceed $60, you receive a cashback for the excess. You need to register for the program through Service NSW.

faq.q: What happens if I drive a toll road without an e-tag?
faq.a: You'll receive a toll notice (video matching via number plate) with a processing fee of $10-$20 on top of the toll. You have a few days to pay. Repeated unpaid tolls lead to penalty notices from Revenue NSW, and fines escalate significantly.

faq.q: Are Brisbane toll roads expensive?
faq.a: Brisbane tolls are generally cheaper than Sydney. The Gateway Motorway costs about $4-5 per trip. Clem7 and Airport Link are $4-6. The Logan Motorway is $3-4. Annual costs for a daily Brisbane commuter using one toll road are typically $2,000-$3,000.

howItWorks: The calculator uses published toll rates for each motorway, adjusted for vehicle class and time of day where applicable. It shows the one-way toll, return trip cost, weekly cost (based on your trips per week), and projected annual cost over 48 working weeks. Where toll caps apply (NSW), the capped weekly amount is shown for comparison.

title: Car Loan Calculator
description: Calculate car loan repayments, total interest, and the cost impact of a balloon payment.
metaDescription: Calculate Australian car loan repayments for any amount, rate and term. See monthly costs, total interest and balloon payment impact.

inputs.label: Loan Amount
inputs.help: The amount you need to finance

inputs.label: Balloon Payment
inputs.help: Large final payment at end of loan — reduces monthly repayments but you owe a lump sum

inputs.label: Trade-In Value
inputs.help: Value of your current car if trading in (reduces loan amount)

faq.q: What are typical car loan rates in Australia?
faq.a: As of early 2026, secured car loan rates from banks and specialist lenders range from about 6.5% to 9.5% p.a. for new cars. Used car loans are typically 0.5-2% higher. Rates depend on credit history, loan term, and the age of the vehicle.

faq.q: What is a balloon payment?
faq.a: A balloon payment is a large lump sum due at the end of the loan term. It reduces your regular repayments but means you owe a significant amount at the end — you either pay it, refinance it, or trade the car in. Common in novated leases and dealer finance.

faq.q: Should I get a car loan or pay cash?
faq.a: Paying cash avoids interest costs. But if your savings earn more than the loan rate (e.g. in an offset account against your mortgage at 6%+), financing the car can sometimes make financial sense. Also consider that cash provides a buffer for emergencies.

faq.q: Is dealer finance a good deal?
faq.a: Dealer finance is convenient but often more expensive than a bank or credit union car loan. Always compare rates. Dealer finance rates of 8-12% are common, while direct lenders often offer 6-8%. The comparison rate (including fees) is the number to compare.

howItWorks: The calculator uses the standard loan amortisation formula adjusted for balloon payments. With a balloon, it computes repayments on (loan amount - balloon present value) over the term, then adds the balloon as a final payment. It shows the per-period repayment, total of all repayments, total interest paid, and the true cost of the car (repayments + balloon + deposit/trade-in).

title: Days Between Dates Calculator
description: Calculate the number of days, weeks, months, and business days between any two dates.
metaDescription: Calculate days between any two dates. See total days, weeks, months and business days between your chosen dates.

inputs.label: State (for Public Holidays)
inputs.help: Used to exclude state-specific public holidays from business day count

faq.q: How are business days calculated?
faq.a: Business days exclude Saturdays and Sundays. When public holidays are also excluded, state-specific public holidays (which vary across Australian states) are removed from the count as well.

faq.q: Why does the business day count matter?
faq.a: Many Australian legal and financial deadlines are counted in business days — for example, cooling-off periods for property contracts (5 business days in QLD), notice periods, settlement timelines, and statutory response deadlines.

faq.q: Do all states have the same public holidays?
faq.a: No. All states share national public holidays (New Year's, Australia Day, Good Friday, Easter Saturday, Easter Monday, Anzac Day, Queen's Birthday, Christmas Day, Boxing Day), but each state has additional unique holidays. For example, QLD has Royal Queensland Show (Brisbane only), VIC has Melbourne Cup Day (metro only).

howItWorks: The calculator counts calendar days between the two dates, optionally including the end date. It then computes weeks and remaining days, approximate months, business days (excluding weekends), and optionally removes state-specific Australian public holidays from the business day count.

title: Age in Days Calculator
description: Calculate your exact age in days, weeks, months, hours, and minutes from your date of birth.
metaDescription: Find out your exact age in days, hours, minutes and seconds. Enter your date of birth and see every time unit of your life.
keywords: how many days old am I

faq.q: How many days is 1,000 days old?
faq.a: 1,000 days is approximately 2 years and 9 months. Many parents celebrate this milestone — it falls roughly when a child turns 2 years 8-9 months.

faq.q: How many days has the average Australian lived?
faq.a: The average Australian life expectancy is about 83 years, which is approximately 30,300 days. If you're 30 years old, you've lived about 10,950 days — roughly one-third of the average lifespan.

faq.q: When will I be 10,000 days old?
faq.a: 10,000 days is about 27 years and 5 months. If you were born on 1 January 2000, your 10,000th day was around 18 May 2027.

howItWorks: The calculator computes the difference between your date of birth and today in multiple units: total days, total weeks, total months, total hours (days x 24), and total minutes (hours x 60). It also identifies upcoming milestones like your 10,000th, 15,000th, or 20,000th day.

title: Time Zone Converter
description: Convert times between Australian time zones and international cities.
metaDescription: Convert times between Australian time zones and cities worldwide. AEST, AEDT, ACST, AWST and international zones.

inputs.label: Time
inputs.help: Enter in 24h or 12h format

inputs.label: Date
inputs.help: Important for daylight saving time changes

faq.q: What are Australia's time zones?
faq.a: Australia has three main time zones: AEST (UTC+10) for QLD, NSW, VIC, TAS, ACT; ACST (UTC+9:30) for SA and NT; and AWST (UTC+8) for WA. NSW, VIC, TAS, SA, and ACT observe daylight saving (AEDT/ACDT) from first Sunday in October to first Sunday in April. QLD, WA, and NT do not observe DST.

faq.q: Does Queensland do daylight saving?
faq.a: No. Queensland does not observe daylight saving time. During DST months, QLD is 1 hour behind Sydney/Melbourne (QLD stays on AEST UTC+10 while they move to AEDT UTC+11). This has been a contentious political issue for decades.

faq.q: What is the time difference between Australia and the US?
faq.a: AEST to US Eastern: 14-16 hours ahead depending on DST. When it's 9am Monday in Sydney, it's roughly 6-7pm Sunday in New York. The exact difference varies because Australian and US DST transitions happen on different dates.

howItWorks: The converter takes your input time, applies the UTC offset of the source time zone, converts to UTC, then applies the target time zone's UTC offset. It accounts for daylight saving time transitions based on the selected date. The result shows the converted time, date (in case it crosses midnight), and the UTC offset difference.

title: Public Holiday Planner
description: See all public holidays and long weekends for your state for any year.
metaDescription: Plan your Australian public holidays and long weekends by state. See all holidays for NSW, VIC, QLD, WA, SA, TAS, ACT and NT.

faq.q: How many public holidays does Australia have?
faq.a: There are 8 national public holidays. Each state adds 1-3 additional state/territory holidays. Total per state ranges from about 10-13 per year. If a public holiday falls on a weekend, the following Monday is usually declared the substitute public holiday.

faq.q: Which state has the most public holidays?
faq.a: The ACT and Tasmania tend to have the most public holidays (around 12-13). Queensland has 11-12 including Royal Queensland Show (Brisbane region only). Victoria has 11-12 including Melbourne Cup Day (metro only).

faq.q: When should I book leave for long weekends?
faq.a: The best long weekend strategies: take the Friday before or Monday after Easter (already a 4-day weekend in most states), bridge Anzac Day (April 25) with a weekend, and the days between Christmas and New Year. Taking 4 days of leave around Easter can give you 10 consecutive days off.

howItWorks: The calculator lists all national and state-specific public holidays for the selected year and state. It identifies long weekends (where a holiday falls on a Monday or Friday, or adjacent to a weekend), and suggests optimal leave-taking strategies to maximise consecutive days off using minimal annual leave days.

title: Work Hours Calculator
description: Calculate daily, weekly, and annual work hours based on your start time, end time, and breaks.
metaDescription: Calculate your daily, weekly and annual work hours. Factor in start time, finish time, breaks and days per week.

inputs.label: Break Duration
inputs.help: Unpaid break time per day

inputs.label: Working Weeks Per Year
inputs.help: 52 minus annual leave and public holidays (typically 48)

faq.q: How many hours is full-time in Australia?
faq.a: Full-time work in Australia is generally defined as 38 hours per week under the Fair Work Act. This is the standard for most modern awards and enterprise agreements, though some industries have different standards.

faq.q: How many work hours are in a year?
faq.a: A full-time worker doing 38 hours/week for 48 weeks (allowing 4 weeks annual leave) works approximately 1,824 hours per year. At 52 weeks (no leave), it's 1,976 hours. These figures are useful for converting between annual salary and hourly rate.

faq.q: Do I get paid for breaks?
faq.a: Under most Australian awards, meal breaks of 30-60 minutes are unpaid. Shorter rest breaks (10-15 minutes) are usually paid. If you're required to remain available during your break, it may be considered paid time. Check your specific award or agreement.

howItWorks: The calculator computes daily hours from your start and end times minus break duration. Weekly hours = daily hours x days per week. Annual hours = weekly hours x working weeks. It also shows the equivalent in minutes for timesheet accuracy and flags whether the weekly total meets the 38-hour full-time standard.

title: Metric-Imperial Converter
description: Convert between metric and imperial units for length, weight, temperature, and volume.
metaDescription: Convert between metric and imperial units. Length, weight, temperature and volume conversions with formulas shown.

faq.q: Does Australia use metric or imperial?
faq.a: Australia officially adopted the metric system in 1966 (currency) and completed conversion by the mid-1970s. All official measurements, speed limits, and product labels use metric. However, many Australians still informally use feet/inches for height and some use pounds for weight.

faq.q: What are the most common conversions?
faq.a: Popular conversions: 1 inch = 2.54 cm, 1 foot = 30.48 cm, 1 mile = 1.609 km, 1 pound = 0.4536 kg, 1 stone = 6.35 kg, 1 gallon (US) = 3.785 L, 1 gallon (imperial) = 4.546 L. Temperature: °F = (°C x 9/5) + 32.

faq.q: Why do some Australians still use imperial?
faq.a: Older Australians who grew up pre-metrication often still think in feet, inches, and pounds. Real estate also uses a mix — room sizes in metres but land in square metres (not acres). Screen sizes are universally in inches. Tyre pressures sometimes use PSI alongside kPa.

howItWorks: The converter applies the standard conversion factor between the selected units. Each conversion is mathematically exact (e.g. 1 inch = exactly 25.4 mm). The conversion formula is shown alongside the result so you can verify or apply it manually. Temperature uses the Celsius/Fahrenheit/Kelvin conversion formulas.

title: Currency Converter
description: Convert between Australian dollars and major world currencies at mid-market exchange rates.
metaDescription: Convert Australian dollars to and from major world currencies. Uses mid-market exchange rates updated regularly.

faq.q: What exchange rate does this use?
faq.a: This converter uses the mid-market rate — the midpoint between buy and sell rates. Banks and currency exchange services add a margin on top of this rate. The actual rate you get will be slightly worse than the mid-market rate shown here.

faq.q: Where do Australians get the best exchange rates?
faq.a: Online currency transfer services (Wise, OFX, Revolut) typically offer rates closest to mid-market. Banks usually add 2-4% margin. Airport currency exchanges are the most expensive — often 5-10% worse than mid-market. Always compare the total cost including fees.

faq.q: Do I need to declare foreign currency when entering Australia?
faq.a: Yes. If you bring AUD $10,000 or more (or foreign currency equivalent) into or out of Australia, you must declare it to Australian Border Force. This applies to cash, traveller's cheques, and bearer negotiable instruments.

howItWorks: The converter multiplies the input amount by the current mid-market exchange rate between the selected currencies. Rates are indicative and sourced from public exchange rate feeds. The result shows the converted amount, the exchange rate used, and the inverse rate. It also shows what typical bank and transfer service rates might look like with their margins.

title: GST Calculator
description: Add GST, remove GST, or find the GST component of any amount — Australian 10% GST.
metaDescription: Calculate Australian GST instantly. Add 10% GST, remove GST from a total, or find the GST component of any amount.

options.label: Find GST in a total (amount is inc-GST)

inputs.label: GST Rate
inputs.help: Australian GST is 10%. Change for other countries.

faq.q: What is the GST rate in Australia?
faq.a: The Australian GST rate is 10%. It applies to most goods and services. GST-free items include basic food (fresh fruit, veg, bread, milk, meat), some health services, education, and exports. Input-taxed supplies include financial services and residential rent.

faq.q: How do I calculate GST?
faq.a: To add GST: multiply by 1.1. To find the GST in a GST-inclusive price: divide by 11. To find the ex-GST amount from a GST-inclusive price: divide by 1.1. For example: $100 ex-GST = $110 inc-GST. The GST component is $10.

faq.q: When do I need to register for GST?
faq.a: You must register for GST if your business has a GST turnover of $75,000 or more per year ($150,000 for non-profit organisations). Taxi/rideshare drivers must register regardless of turnover. Registration is done through the ATO when you get your ABN.

faq.q: What items are GST-free in Australia?
faq.a: Basic food (not prepared/restaurant food), most medical and health services, some educational courses, childcare, exports, international transport, precious metals, and some water and sewerage services. Residential rent is 'input-taxed' — no GST is charged but the landlord can't claim GST credits.

howItWorks: Adding GST: amount x 1.10 = GST-inclusive total. Removing GST: amount / 1.10 = ex-GST amount. Finding GST component: amount / 11 = GST amount. The calculator shows all three values (ex-GST, GST component, inc-GST total) regardless of which direction you choose.

title: Percentage Calculator
description: Calculate percentages three ways — X% of Y, X is what % of Y, and percentage change between two values.
metaDescription: Calculate percentages easily. Find X% of a number, what percentage one number is of another, or the percentage change between values.

faq.q: How do I calculate a percentage?
faq.a: Three common calculations: (1) X% of Y = (X/100) x Y. Example: 15% of 200 = 30. (2) X is what % of Y = (X/Y) x 100. Example: 30 is 15% of 200. (3) % change = ((new - old) / old) x 100. Example: 200 to 230 = +15% change.

faq.q: How do I calculate a discount?
faq.a: To find the discounted price: original price x (1 - discount%/100). For a 25% discount on $80: $80 x 0.75 = $60. The discount amount is $80 x 0.25 = $20.

faq.q: What is the difference between percentage and percentage points?
faq.a: A move from 10% to 15% is a 5 percentage point increase, but a 50% increase in relative terms. This distinction matters in finance and statistics — 'interest rates rose 0.25 percentage points' is very different from 'interest rates rose 0.25%'.

howItWorks: Mode 1 (X% of Y): multiplies Y by X/100. Mode 2 (X is what % of Y): divides X by Y and multiplies by 100. Mode 3 (% change from X to Y): calculates (Y - X) / X x 100, showing whether the change is positive (increase) or negative (decrease). Results are shown to 2 decimal places.

title: Unit Converter
description: Convert between hundreds of units across length, weight, volume, area, speed, pressure, energy, and more.
metaDescription: Universal unit converter for length, weight, volume, area, speed, pressure, energy, data and more. Quick and accurate conversions.

inputs.label: From Unit
inputs.help: Options change based on category

inputs.label: To Unit
inputs.help: Options change based on category

faq.q: What unit categories are available?
faq.a: This converter covers: length (mm to miles), weight (mg to tonnes), volume (mL to gallons), area (cm² to acres), speed (km/h to knots), temperature (°C, °F, K), pressure (kPa, PSI, bar, atm), energy (kJ, kcal, Wh, BTU), digital storage (bytes to TB), time, angles, and frequency.

faq.q: Is a US gallon the same as an imperial gallon?
faq.a: No. A US gallon is 3.785 litres, while an Imperial gallon is 4.546 litres. Australia historically used Imperial gallons before metrication. US recipes and fuel economy figures use US gallons. This converter distinguishes between the two.

faq.q: How many square metres in an acre?
faq.a: 1 acre = 4,046.86 m² (roughly 0.4 hectares). 1 hectare = 10,000 m² = 2.471 acres. Australian property land sizes are typically listed in square metres or hectares.

howItWorks: The converter stores precise conversion factors for every unit relative to a base unit in each category (e.g. metres for length, kilograms for weight). To convert, it divides the input by the 'from' unit's factor and multiplies by the 'to' unit's factor. Temperature uses separate formulas. The result shows the conversion and the formula used.

title: Cooking Measurement Converter
description: Convert between cups, tablespoons, teaspoons, millilitres, grams, and ounces for cooking.
metaDescription: Convert cooking measurements between cups, tablespoons, teaspoons, mL, grams and ounces. Australian metric cup (250mL) used.

inputs.label: Ingredient (for volume to weight)
inputs.help: Required when converting between volume and weight

faq.q: Is an Australian cup the same as a US cup?
faq.a: No. An Australian metric cup is 250 mL. A US cup is approximately 236.6 mL. An Australian tablespoon is 20 mL (4 teaspoons), while a US tablespoon is 15 mL (3 teaspoons). This matters when using American recipes in Australia.

faq.q: How many grams is a cup of flour?
faq.a: One Australian cup (250mL) of plain flour weighs approximately 150g. Self-raising flour is similar at about 150g. Wholemeal flour is about 160g per cup. These are approximate — flour density varies with how packed it is.

faq.q: Why do I need to specify the ingredient?
faq.a: Volume measurements (cups, mL) and weight measurements (grams, ounces) are different things. A cup of flour weighs much less than a cup of sugar because they have different densities. The ingredient selection applies the correct density for accurate conversion.

howItWorks: For volume-to-volume conversions, the calculator uses standard metric conversion factors (1 AU cup = 250 mL, 1 AU tbsp = 20 mL, 1 tsp = 5 mL). For volume-to-weight and weight-to-volume, it applies the density of the selected ingredient (e.g. flour ~0.6 g/mL, sugar ~0.85 g/mL, water 1.0 g/mL). The result is rounded to a practical cooking precision.

title: Tip Calculator
description: Calculate the tip amount and split the bill between friends.
metaDescription: Calculate tips and split bills easily. Choose your tip percentage and number of people to see per-person amounts.

faq.q: How much should I tip in Australia?
faq.a: Tipping is not expected or required in Australia — service workers receive a fair minimum wage. If you want to reward good service, 5-10% is generous. Some restaurants add a surcharge on weekends and public holidays (10-15%) which is not a tip.

faq.q: Is tipping different in Australia vs the US?
faq.a: Very different. In the US, tips of 15-20% are expected because servers are paid below minimum wage. In Australia, the minimum wage is much higher (currently $24.10/hour for adults) and tipping is entirely optional.

faq.q: What are restaurant surcharges in Australia?
faq.a: Many Australian restaurants add surcharges on Sundays (10%), public holidays (10-15%), and sometimes Saturdays (5-10%). Some add a credit card surcharge (1-2%). These are separate from tips and are legal if clearly disclosed on the menu.

howItWorks: Tip amount = bill x (tip percentage / 100). Total = bill + tip. Per person = total / number of people. If rounding is selected, the per-person amount is rounded up to the nearest dollar or $5, and the new total and tip are recalculated accordingly.

title: Annual Leave Calculator
description: Calculate accrued annual leave based on your employment period, hours, and leave loading.
metaDescription: Calculate your annual leave entitlement in Australia. See accrued leave hours, days and dollar value with leave loading.

inputs.label: End Date (or Today)
inputs.help: Leave blank for accrual to today

inputs.label: Leave Loading
inputs.help: Standard leave loading is 17.5% — check your award or agreement

inputs.label: Hourly Rate (Optional)
inputs.help: For calculating the dollar value of accrued leave

inputs.label: Leave Already Taken (Hours)
inputs.help: Hours of annual leave you've already used

faq.q: How much annual leave do I accrue in Australia?
faq.a: Full-time employees accrue 4 weeks (152 hours based on 38hrs/week) of paid annual leave per year under the National Employment Standards. Part-time employees accrue leave on a pro-rata basis. Casual employees do not accrue annual leave (it's built into their casual loading).

faq.q: What is leave loading?
faq.a: Leave loading is an extra payment (typically 17.5%) on top of your base pay while on annual leave. Not all employees receive it — check your award, enterprise agreement, or contract. Some modern awards and agreements have abolished it in favour of higher base pay.

faq.q: Can I cash out annual leave?
faq.a: Under the Fair Work Act, you can cash out annual leave if it's permitted by your award or agreement, a written agreement is made each time, and you retain at least 4 weeks of accrued leave after the cashout. Not all awards allow it.

faq.q: Does annual leave accrue during parental leave?
faq.a: Annual leave does not accrue during periods of unpaid parental leave. It does accrue during paid personal/carer's leave, paid annual leave, community service leave, and long service leave.

howItWorks: Annual leave accrues at a rate of 4 weeks per year of service (pro-rata for part-time based on ordinary hours). The calculator counts the total weeks of service between your start and end dates, multiplies by (4/52) weeks of leave per week worked, converts to hours based on your weekly hours, then subtracts any leave already taken. Leave loading is applied to show the dollar value.

title: Long Service Leave Calculator
description: Calculate your long service leave entitlement by state, years of service, and pay rate.
metaDescription: Calculate your Australian long service leave entitlement. Varies by state — see your accrued LSL based on years of service and pay rate.

inputs.label: Current Weekly Pay
inputs.help: Your current ordinary weekly pay rate (used to calculate payout value)

faq.q: When do I become entitled to long service leave?
faq.a: In most states, you're entitled to LSL after 10 years of continuous service with the same employer. In VIC and QLD, pro-rata access is available after 7 years if your employment ends. The ACT grants entitlement after 7 years. Exact rules vary by state and award.

faq.q: How much long service leave do I get?
faq.a: The standard entitlement is 8.6667 weeks (2 months) after 10 years in most states. This equates to roughly 0.8667 weeks per year of service. Some states and awards provide more generous entitlements — for example, the ACT provides 6.0667 days per year.

faq.q: Is long service leave paid out on termination?
faq.a: Yes, in most states. If you've completed the qualifying period (usually 7-10 years depending on state), untaken LSL must be paid out on termination regardless of the reason. Some states pay pro-rata after 5-7 years in certain termination scenarios.

faq.q: Does LSL accrue during other leave?
faq.a: Yes. Long service leave accrues during paid leave (annual leave, personal leave, parental leave on pay). It generally does not accrue during unpaid leave, although this can vary by award or agreement.

howItWorks: The calculator applies the selected state's long service leave legislation to determine your accrued entitlement. Each state specifies a number of weeks of leave per period of service (e.g. 8.6667 weeks after 10 years). The calculator converts this to hours based on your weekly hours, and to a dollar value using your current pay rate. Pro-rata entitlements for service between 7-10 years are shown where applicable.

title: Redundancy Pay Calculator
description: Calculate your minimum redundancy pay entitlement based on years of continuous service.
metaDescription: Calculate your Australian redundancy pay entitlement. See minimum severance pay based on years of service under the Fair Work Act.

inputs.label: Weekly Base Pay
inputs.help: Ordinary weekly pay excluding overtime, penalties, allowances

inputs.label: Are You Over 45?
inputs.help: Employees over 45 with 2+ years of service get an extra week of notice

faq.q: How is redundancy pay calculated in Australia?
faq.a: Under the Fair Work Act, minimum redundancy pay is: 1 year = 4 weeks, 2 years = 6 weeks, 3 years = 7 weeks, 4 years = 8 weeks, 5 years = 10 weeks, 6-7 years = 11-12 weeks, 8-9 years = 13-14 weeks, 10+ years = 12 weeks. Based on the employee's base rate of pay for ordinary hours.

faq.q: Are all employees entitled to redundancy pay?
faq.a: No. Redundancy pay under the NES applies to employees of businesses with 15 or more employees. Small businesses (fewer than 15 employees) are exempt from NES redundancy pay. Casual employees and those employed for less than 12 months are also not entitled. Some awards may provide different entitlements.

faq.q: What else do I get paid on redundancy?
faq.a: In addition to redundancy pay, you're entitled to: payment in lieu of notice (1-5 weeks depending on service and age), accrued annual leave, accrued long service leave (if eligible), and pay for any hours worked in the final pay period.

faq.q: Can my employer reduce redundancy pay?
faq.a: An employer can apply to the Fair Work Commission to reduce redundancy pay if they find the employee other acceptable employment, or cannot afford to pay the full amount. The Commission assesses each case on its merits.

howItWorks: The calculator looks up the NES minimum redundancy pay scale based on years of continuous service, and multiplies the applicable number of weeks by the employee's base weekly pay rate. It also calculates the notice period entitlement (1-5 weeks based on service length, plus 1 week if over 45 with 2+ years service). The total payout combines redundancy pay and notice pay.

title: Notice Period Calculator
description: Calculate the minimum notice period for termination of employment under the Fair Work Act.
metaDescription: Calculate the minimum notice period for ending employment in Australia. Based on years of service and age under Fair Work Act.

inputs.label: Employee Over 45?
inputs.help: Employees over 45 with 2+ years of service get an extra week of notice

inputs.label: Weekly Pay (Optional)
inputs.help: To calculate payment in lieu of notice

faq.q: What is the minimum notice period in Australia?
faq.a: Under the Fair Work Act NES: up to 1 year of service = 1 week notice, 1-3 years = 2 weeks, 3-5 years = 3 weeks, 5+ years = 4 weeks. Add 1 extra week if the employee is over 45 and has completed at least 2 years of service.

faq.q: Does the employer have to let me work through the notice period?
faq.a: No. The employer can choose to pay you in lieu of notice — they pay you for the notice period but your employment ends immediately. They can also direct you to use annual leave during the notice period (with certain restrictions).

faq.q: What if my award or contract specifies a longer notice period?
faq.a: The NES sets the minimum. Your award, enterprise agreement, or employment contract may provide a longer notice period. The more generous entitlement applies. Some senior roles have 3-6 month notice periods by contract.

faq.q: Do casual employees get notice?
faq.a: No. Casual employees are not entitled to notice of termination under the NES. However, an employer must not terminate a casual employee for an unlawful reason (e.g. discrimination, exercising a workplace right).

howItWorks: The calculator determines the minimum notice period weeks based on the NES scale: 1 week (under 1 year), 2 weeks (1-3 years), 3 weeks (3-5 years), or 4 weeks (5+ years). If the employee is over 45 with at least 2 years of service, 1 additional week is added. The payment in lieu amount is calculated by multiplying the weekly pay by the number of notice weeks.

title: Hourly Rate Calculator
description: Convert an annual salary to hourly, daily, weekly, and fortnightly rates.
metaDescription: Convert your annual salary to an hourly rate. See daily, weekly and fortnightly pay from your Australian salary.

inputs.label: Hours Per Week
inputs.help: Standard full-time is 38 hours

inputs.label: Working Weeks Per Year
inputs.help: 52 weeks for a standard year (leave is paid)

faq.q: How do I convert salary to hourly rate?
faq.a: Hourly rate = annual salary / (hours per week x weeks per year). For $85,000 at 38 hours/week over 52 weeks: $85,000 / (38 x 52) = $85,000 / 1,976 = $43.02/hour.

faq.q: What is the minimum wage in Australia?
faq.a: As of 1 July 2024, the national minimum wage is $24.10 per hour or $915.91 per 38-hour week. Award rates for specific industries are generally higher than the national minimum. The Fair Work Commission reviews the minimum wage annually, with the next increase effective 1 July 2025.

faq.q: How do I compare a contract rate to a salary?
faq.a: When comparing, remember that a salary typically includes annual leave (4 weeks), personal leave (10 days), superannuation (11.5%), and sometimes other benefits. A contractor charging $80/hour might be equivalent to a salary of $100-120k once you factor in leave, super, and business costs the contractor covers.

faq.q: Does 52 weeks or 48 weeks matter?
faq.a: Use 52 weeks when your salary covers the full year (including paid leave), which is the standard for employment contracts. Use 48 weeks only if you want to calculate an 'actual working' hourly rate that reflects only the weeks you're physically at work.

howItWorks: The calculator divides your annual salary by total annual hours (hours/week x weeks/year) to get the hourly rate. If super is inclusive, it first extracts the base salary by dividing by (1 + SG rate). It then shows: hourly, daily (hourly x daily hours), weekly, fortnightly, monthly (annual / 12), and annual equivalents for easy comparison.

title: Overtime Pay Calculator
description: Calculate overtime pay at 1.5x and 2x rates including public holiday loading under Australian workplace law.
metaDescription: Calculate Australian overtime pay at time-and-a-half and double-time rates. Includes public holiday loading and weekly total pay.

inputs.label: Base Hourly Rate
inputs.help: Your ordinary hourly rate before penalties

inputs.label: Normal Hours per Week
inputs.help: Standard hours before overtime kicks in

inputs.label: Overtime Hours at 1.5x
inputs.help: Hours at time-and-a-half rate

inputs.label: Overtime Hours at 2x
inputs.help: Hours at double-time rate

inputs.label: Public Holiday Hours
inputs.help: Hours worked on a public holiday (typically 2.5x)

inputs.label: Public Holiday Rate
inputs.help: Check your award or enterprise agreement

faq.q: What is the standard overtime rate in Australia?
faq.a: Under most modern awards, the first 2-3 hours of overtime are paid at 1.5x (time and a half) and anything after that at 2x (double time). Check your specific award on the Fair Work Ombudsman website.

faq.q: How much do public holidays pay in Australia?
faq.a: Most awards pay 2.5x the base rate for hours worked on a public holiday, plus you're entitled to the day off if you don't work. Some awards pay 2x or 3x — check your specific award.

faq.q: Is overtime compulsory in Australia?
faq.a: An employer can request overtime, but an employee can refuse if it's unreasonable. Factors include notice, health, family responsibilities, and the employee's circumstances under the Fair Work Act 2009.

faq.q: Do casual employees get overtime?
faq.a: Yes. Casual employees are entitled to overtime rates on top of their casual loading (typically 25%) once they exceed the maximum ordinary hours specified in their award.

howItWorks: Multiplies your base hourly rate by each penalty rate tier (1.5x, 2x, public holiday multiplier) for the hours in each tier, then sums all components to give your total weekly pay and total overtime earnings.

faq.q: What is a break-even point?
faq.a: The break-even point is when your total revenue equals your total costs (startup + ongoing). After this point, the business is generating profit. Most small businesses in Australia take 1-3 years to break even.

faq.q: How much cash reserve should a new business have?
faq.a: The Australian Small Business and Family Enterprise Ombudsman recommends 3-6 months of operating expenses as a cash buffer. For seasonal businesses, 6-12 months is safer.

faq.q: What are common startup costs in Australia?
faq.a: Common costs include ABN/business registration ($0-$500), business insurance ($1,000-5,000/yr), equipment, website, marketing, fit-out, legal advice, accounting setup, and initial inventory. The ABS reports average startup costs of $10,000-$50,000 for small businesses.

faq.q: Do I need an ABN to start a business?
faq.a: You need an ABN if your annual turnover is $75,000 or more (or you're running an enterprise). You must register for GST if turnover hits $75,000. ABN registration is free at abr.gov.au.

howItWorks: Calculates monthly net cashflow (revenue minus expenses), applies the growth rate to project future revenue, and determines when cumulative profit covers startup costs. Also shows runway — how many months your cash reserve lasts if revenue falls short.

faq.q: What is the fuel tax credit rate?
faq.a: As of 2024-25, the rate for heavy vehicles on public roads is approximately 18.8 cents per litre. Off-road and primary production use gets a higher credit (around 48.8 cents/L) because full excise is refunded. Rates change every 6 months — check ato.gov.au for current rates.

faq.q: Who can claim fuel tax credits?
faq.a: Businesses registered for GST and fuel tax credits that use fuel in machinery, heavy vehicles, light vehicles for business, or power generation. You cannot claim for fuel in light vehicles under 4.5 tonnes used on public roads.

faq.q: How do I claim fuel tax credits?
faq.a: Claim on your BAS (Business Activity Statement) at label 7D. You need to keep records of fuel purchases (receipts or fuel cards) and be able to show the fuel was used for an eligible business purpose.

faq.q: Can I claim fuel tax credits for utes?
faq.a: Only if the ute's gross vehicle mass exceeds 4.5 tonnes. Most single-cab and dual-cab utes are under this limit and don't qualify for on-road fuel tax credits. However, fuel used off-road (e.g., on a farm) may still be eligible.

howItWorks: Multiplies the litres of fuel used by the applicable ATO fuel tax credit rate for your fuel type and activity. Rates differ between on-road and off-road use, and between heavy and light vehicles. The result is the credit amount you can claim on your BAS.

faq.q: What is the average super balance by age in Australia?
faq.a: ABS data shows median super balances: age 30-34 ~$45,000, age 40-44 ~$90,000, age 50-54 ~$155,000, age 60-64 ~$210,000. These are medians — averages are higher due to high-balance outliers.

faq.q: What is the super guarantee rate?
faq.a: The SG rate is 11.5% for 2024-25 and will increase to 12% from 1 July 2025, where it is legislated to stay.

faq.q: How much super do I need to retire?
faq.a: ASFA's Retirement Standard (March 2024) suggests a single person needs ~$595,000 and a couple ~$690,000 for a comfortable retirement. This assumes owning a home and supplementing with the Age Pension.

faq.q: What is the concessional contributions cap?
faq.a: The concessional (pre-tax) cap is $30,000 per year for 2024-25. This includes employer SG, salary sacrifice, and personal deductible contributions. Unused cap amounts can be carried forward for up to 5 years if your total super balance is under $500,000.

howItWorks: Projects your super balance year-by-year by adding employer SG contributions and salary sacrifice to the running balance, then applying the expected annual investment return. Shows the projected balance at your chosen retirement age in today's dollars (not inflation-adjusted).

faq.q: What is the ASFA Comfortable Retirement Standard?
faq.a: ASFA's Comfortable Retirement Standard (2024) requires ~$51,630/year for singles and ~$72,663/year for couples. This covers a good standard of living including health insurance, a reasonable car, leisure activities, and occasional overseas travel.

faq.q: Will I get the Age Pension?
faq.a: The full Age Pension (2024) is ~$28,514/year for singles and ~$42,988/year for couples. It's means-tested on assets and income. Many retirees receive a part pension. The pension age is 67.

faq.q: How long does super last in retirement?
faq.a: ASFA suggests a lump sum of ~$595,000 (single) or ~$690,000 (couple) at age 67 for a comfortable retirement, assuming you own your home. The 4% rule suggests drawing down 4% per year, making your super last ~25 years.

faq.q: Should I use super or savings first?
faq.a: Generally, drawing on non-super savings first can be advantageous because super in pension phase is tax-free. However, this depends on your specific tax situation and age — consult a financial adviser for personalised advice.

howItWorks: Projects your combined super and savings at retirement age using compound growth, then calculates the total income needed over your retirement years. The gap (if any) is the shortfall between projected savings and the amount needed to fund your desired retirement income.

faq.q: How much do super fees really cost?
faq.a: A seemingly small difference of 0.5% in fees can cost over $100,000 over a 30-year period on a typical balance. The Productivity Commission found Australians lose $2.6 billion per year to underperforming super funds.

faq.q: What is a good super fund fee in Australia?
faq.a: Industry funds typically charge 0.5-0.8% in total fees. Retail funds average 1.0-1.5%. The cheapest options (like indexed options) can be under 0.15%. The APRA heatmap rates funds on fees and performance.

faq.q: Should I consolidate my super?
faq.a: Consolidating into one fund eliminates duplicate fees and insurance premiums. Check for any exit fees or loss of insurance before rolling over. You can consolidate via myGov linked to the ATO.

faq.q: Are industry super funds better than retail funds?
faq.a: APRA data consistently shows industry funds outperform retail funds on average, primarily due to lower fees. However, individual fund selection matters more than fund type. Check APRA's annual fund-level performance data.

howItWorks: Projects each fund's balance over 10, 20, and 30 years by applying gross returns, subtracting percentage-based fees, fixed fees, and insurance costs annually. Contributions are added each year. The comparison shows the dollar difference between funds at each time horizon.

title: Super Contribution Optimiser

faq.q: What is the concessional contributions cap?
faq.a: The concessional (pre-tax) cap is $30,000 per year for 2024-25. This includes employer SG, salary sacrifice, and personal deductible contributions. Exceeding the cap means the excess is taxed at your marginal rate plus an interest charge.

faq.q: What is the carry-forward rule?
faq.a: If your total super balance is under $500,000 at 30 June of the previous year, you can use unused concessional cap amounts from the past 5 years. This is useful for making large catch-up contributions.

faq.q: How much tax do I save with salary sacrifice?
faq.a: Concessional contributions are taxed at 15% in the fund (or 30% if your income exceeds $250,000). If your marginal rate is 32.5%, sacrificing $10,000 saves you $1,750 in tax ($10,000 x (32.5% - 15%)).

faq.q: What is the non-concessional cap?
faq.a: The non-concessional (after-tax) cap is $120,000 per year for 2024-25. If you're under 75, you can bring forward up to 3 years ($360,000) in one year. This is separate from the concessional cap.

howItWorks: Calculates total concessional contributions (employer SG + salary sacrifice + personal deductible) and checks against the $30,000 cap plus any carried-forward amounts. Shows the tax saved by comparing marginal tax on the contributed amount versus the 15% super tax rate.

title: Compound Interest Calculator
description: Calculate compound interest with regular contributions, showing growth over time with different compounding frequencies.
metaDescription: Free compound interest calculator with regular contributions. See how your savings grow with daily, monthly, or yearly compounding.

faq.q: What is compound interest?
faq.a: Compound interest is interest calculated on both the initial principal and the accumulated interest from previous periods. Albert Einstein allegedly called it 'the eighth wonder of the world' — the key is that your money earns returns on its returns.

faq.q: How does compounding frequency affect growth?
faq.a: More frequent compounding produces slightly higher returns. For example, $10,000 at 6% for 20 years: annually = $32,071; monthly = $33,102; daily = $33,198. The difference is most noticeable at higher rates and longer time periods.

faq.q: What is a good savings interest rate in Australia?
faq.a: As of 2024-25, high-interest savings accounts in Australia offer 4.5-5.5% for balances meeting bonus conditions. Term deposits offer 4.0-5.0% depending on term length. Compare rates at the RBA or comparison sites.

faq.q: How does the Rule of 72 work?
faq.a: Divide 72 by your annual interest rate to estimate how many years it takes to double your money. At 6%, that is 72/6 = 12 years. At 8%, it is 72/8 = 9 years. It is an approximation but remarkably accurate.

howItWorks: Uses the compound interest formula A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt) plus a future value of annuity calculation for regular contributions. The compounding frequency (n) determines how often interest is calculated and added to the principal. Shows a year-by-year growth table.

faq.q: What is the average credit card interest rate in Australia?
faq.a: The average purchase rate in Australia is approximately 20% p.a. Low-rate cards offer 12-14%, while rewards cards can charge 20-22%. The RBA publishes monthly credit card statistics.

faq.q: How long does it take to pay off a $5,000 credit card?
faq.a: Paying only the 2% minimum on a $5,000 balance at 20% interest takes over 30 years and costs about $9,000 in interest. Adding $100/month extra reduces it to about 3 years and ~$1,500 in interest.

faq.q: Should I do a balance transfer?
faq.a: Balance transfers (often 0% for 12-24 months) can save significant interest if you commit to paying off the balance within the promotional period. Watch out for balance transfer fees (1-3%) and the revert rate (often 22%+).

faq.q: Does closing a credit card affect my credit score?
faq.a: Closing a card can slightly reduce your available credit, which may affect your score. In Australia, the credit reporting system (comprehensive credit reporting) records account openings and closings. The impact is usually minor if you have other credit.

howItWorks: Simulates month-by-month repayments using the greater of the percentage-based minimum or dollar floor. Interest is calculated on the daily balance method (rate/365 x days x balance). Extra payments are applied directly to the principal, dramatically reducing total interest.

faq.q: How much can I borrow on a $100,000 salary?
faq.a: As a rough guide, banks lend 5-6x your gross income for a single applicant with no debts. On $100k, that is roughly $500k-$600k. However, actual borrowing power depends heavily on expenses, debts, dependants, and the assessment rate banks use (typically 3% above the actual rate).

faq.q: What deposit do I need for a home loan?
faq.a: A 20% deposit avoids Lenders Mortgage Insurance (LMI). With 5-10% deposit, you can still borrow but will pay LMI ($5,000-$30,000+). Some lenders offer 5% deposit loans. First Home Guarantee lets eligible buyers buy with 5% and no LMI.

faq.q: What is the serviceability buffer?
faq.a: APRA requires banks to assess borrowers at an interest rate at least 3 percentage points above the loan product rate. So if your rate is 6.5%, the bank tests whether you can afford repayments at 9.5%.

faq.q: Do lenders use HEM or my actual expenses?
faq.a: Since the royal commission, most lenders use the higher of your declared expenses or the Household Expenditure Measure (HEM) benchmark. HEM is a modest living standard based on household size. Being transparent about expenses is important.

howItWorks: Calculates maximum affordable repayments based on 30% of gross income minus existing debts and a living expense allowance (adjusted for dependants). Then works backwards from repayment to maximum loan using the bank assessment rate (actual rate + 3% buffer). Maximum property price is loan plus deposit minus purchase costs.

faq.q: What is the average electricity bill in Australia?
faq.a: The average Australian household pays $1,500-$2,000/year on electricity. This varies significantly by state: SA is typically highest (~$2,100), QLD and NSW around $1,600-1,800, and TAS lowest at ~$1,400. Air conditioning and pool pumps are the biggest variable costs.

faq.q: What is the average kWh usage per household?
faq.a: Average Australian household uses about 15-20 kWh/day (5,500-7,300 kWh/year). A 1-person household uses ~8 kWh/day, while a family of 4 uses ~20 kWh/day. Homes with pools, ducted AC, or electric hot water use substantially more.

faq.q: Which state has the cheapest electricity?
faq.a: As of 2024-25, the ACT and Tasmania generally have the lowest electricity prices, while South Australia and Queensland have the highest. Prices vary significantly between retailers and plans within each state.

faq.q: How much do solar panels save on electricity?
faq.a: A typical 6.6 kW system in Queensland generates ~27 kWh/day and can reduce bills by $1,200-$1,800/year depending on self-consumption and feed-in tariff. Northern states get more solar production than southern states.

howItWorks: Estimates daily kWh usage based on household size, state climate, and major appliances (AC, pool, solar offset). Multiplies by your tariff rate, adds the daily supply charge, and projects to a quarterly bill. Solar offset reduces usage based on system size and state irradiance.
