faq.q: How does the 50% CGT discount work?
faq.a: If you hold a CGT asset for at least 12 months before selling, only 50% of the capital gain is added to your taxable income. For example, a $100,000 gain becomes $50,000 taxable. This discount is not available for companies.

faq.q: Is my home subject to CGT?
faq.a: Your main residence (the home you live in) is generally exempt from CGT under the main residence exemption. However, if you rented it out for part of the time, used it for business, or own land over 2 hectares, a partial CGT liability may apply.

faq.q: How is crypto taxed in Australia?
faq.a: The ATO treats cryptocurrency as a CGT asset. Selling, trading, or using crypto to buy goods triggers a CGT event. The 50% discount applies if held 12+ months. Personal use crypto purchases under $10,000 may be exempt.

faq.q: Can I offset capital losses?
faq.a: Yes. Capital losses can be offset against capital gains in the same year, and unused losses can be carried forward indefinitely. However, capital losses cannot be offset against your regular income.

howItWorks: Calculates the capital gain (sale price minus cost base minus selling costs), applies the 50% discount if held 12+ months, then taxes the net gain at your marginal rate. Also shows the effective CGT rate on the full gain and the tax payable.

title: Division 7A Loan Calculator
description: Calculate the minimum yearly repayment on a Division 7A loan from your company at the ATO benchmark rate.
metaDescription: Calculate Division 7A minimum yearly repayment for loans from your Australian private company. Uses ATO benchmark interest rate.

faq.q: What is Division 7A?
faq.a: Division 7A of the Income Tax Assessment Act 1936 prevents shareholders or associates from accessing company profits tax-free through loans or other payments. If a private company makes a loan to a shareholder, it must be on compliant terms or be treated as an unfranked dividend.

faq.q: What is the ATO benchmark interest rate for Div 7A?
faq.a: The benchmark rate for 2024-25 is 8.27%. This rate changes annually and is published by the ATO before the start of each financial year. The loan must charge interest at or above this rate.

faq.q: What happens if I miss the minimum repayment?
faq.a: If the minimum yearly repayment is not made by the lodgement day of the company's tax return, the shortfall is treated as an unfranked dividend to the borrower and included in their personal assessable income.

faq.q: Can I repay a Division 7A loan early?
faq.a: Yes. There is no penalty for early repayment. The minimum yearly repayment is the floor — you can always repay more. The earlier you repay, the less total interest you pay.

howItWorks: Calculates the minimum yearly repayment using an annuity formula based on the ATO benchmark interest rate and the loan term (7 years unsecured, 25 years secured). The repayment covers both principal and interest and must be made before the company's tax return lodgement date.

title: FBT Car Calculator
description: Calculate Fringe Benefits Tax on a company car using the statutory formula or operating cost method.

faq.q: What is the FBT statutory fraction?
faq.a: The statutory fraction is a flat 20% regardless of kilometres driven (since 2014). The taxable value is 20% x car's cost price x (days available / 365) minus any employee contribution.

faq.q: When is the FBT year?
faq.a: The FBT year runs from 1 April to 31 March — it is different from the financial year. FBT returns are due by 21 May (or later if lodged through a tax agent).

faq.q: What is the FBT rate?
faq.a: The FBT rate for 2024-25 is 47%. This is applied to the grossed-up taxable value. The type 1 gross-up rate (where GST credits are claimed) is 2.0802, and type 2 (no GST credits) is 1.8868.

faq.q: Which FBT method is better for cars?
faq.a: If you do high business kilometres (over 70-80%), the operating cost method usually gives a lower FBT liability. If mostly private use, the statutory method may be similar or simpler. You need a valid 12-week logbook for the operating cost method.

howItWorks: Statutory method: 20% x car cost x (days available / days in year) - employee contribution. Operating cost method: total running costs x private use percentage. Both results are then grossed up and taxed at the FBT rate of 47%.

title: Simple Interest Calculator
description: Calculate simple interest on a principal amount over a set period — no compounding.
metaDescription: Calculate simple interest on savings or loans. See interest earned and total amount with no compounding — basic and quick.

faq.q: What is simple interest?
faq.a: Simple interest is calculated only on the original principal, not on accumulated interest. The formula is I = P x R x T, where P is principal, R is rate (as decimal), and T is time in years.

faq.q: Where is simple interest used?
faq.a: Simple interest is used for some personal loans, car loans, and short-term lending. Most savings accounts and home loans use compound interest instead.

faq.q: What is the difference between simple and compound interest?
faq.a: Simple interest is calculated only on the original principal. Compound interest is calculated on the principal plus any previously earned interest. Over time, compound interest grows much faster.

howItWorks: Uses the formula I = P x r x t where P is the principal, r is the annual rate as a decimal, and t is the time in years. For months, divides by 12; for days, divides by 365. Returns total interest earned and the final total (principal + interest).

title: Loan Comparison Calculator
description: Compare up to three loans side-by-side on repayments, total interest, and total cost.
metaDescription: Compare up to 3 loans side-by-side. See monthly repayments, total interest, and total cost for Australian home loans or personal loans.

faq.q: What is the comparison rate?
faq.a: The comparison rate includes both the interest rate and most fees/charges, expressed as a single percentage. Australian lenders are required to display comparison rates based on a $150,000 loan over 25 years. It helps compare the true cost of loans.

faq.q: Fixed or variable — which is better?
faq.a: Variable rates move with the market (currently higher but may drop). Fixed rates give certainty for 1-5 years but may have restrictions on extra repayments. Many borrowers split their loan (e.g., 50/50 fixed and variable).

faq.q: Do loan fees matter much?
faq.a: A $395/year annual fee on a $500,000 loan adds about 0.08% to your effective rate. On smaller loans, fees matter more. Always compare total cost (repayments + fees) over the loan life, not just the interest rate.

howItWorks: Calculates monthly repayments for each loan using the standard amortisation formula, then adds annual fees to give total yearly costs. Compares total interest paid, total cost (principal + interest + fees), and monthly repayments side by side.

title: Offset Account Savings Calculator
description: Calculate how much interest and time an offset account saves on your home loan.
metaDescription: Calculate how much an Australian mortgage offset account saves in interest and time. See the impact of different offset balances.

inputs.label: Offset Account Balance

faq.q: How does a mortgage offset account work?
faq.a: An offset account is a transaction account linked to your home loan. The balance in the offset reduces the loan balance that interest is charged on. If your loan is $500,000 and your offset has $30,000, you only pay interest on $470,000.

faq.q: Offset account vs extra repayments?
faq.a: Both save the same amount of interest. The key difference is accessibility — offset funds can be withdrawn anytime, while extra repayments into the loan may need a redraw facility and could be restricted with fixed-rate loans.

faq.q: Is an offset account worth the higher fee?
faq.a: Offset accounts typically come with packages costing $300-$400/year. If your offset balance saves more interest than the fee costs, it is worth it. At 6.5%, even a $10,000 balance saves $650/year — well above the fee.

faq.q: Can I have multiple offset accounts?
faq.a: Some lenders offer multiple offset sub-accounts linked to one loan. This can help with budgeting (e.g., separate accounts for bills, savings, emergency fund) while all balances offset your loan interest.

howItWorks: Calculates the loan with and without the offset balance. Interest is charged on (loan balance minus offset balance) each period. The saved interest is the difference between the two scenarios. Also shows how many years earlier you pay off the loan by maintaining the offset.

title: Refinance Savings Calculator

inputs.help: Keep same term to compare fairly, or extend to lower repayments

inputs.label: Switching Costs
inputs.help: Discharge fee, application fee, valuation, legal (typically $700-$3,000)

faq.q: What are typical refinancing costs in Australia?
faq.a: Typical costs include: discharge fee from current lender ($150-$400), settlement/legal fee ($200-$500), new lender application fee ($0-$600), property valuation ($0-$400). Many lenders offer cashback ($2,000-$4,000) which can offset these costs.

faq.q: When is it worth refinancing?
faq.a: As a rule of thumb, if you can save 0.5% or more on your interest rate, refinancing usually pays off within 6-12 months. The bigger your loan and the larger the rate difference, the faster you break even on switching costs.

faq.q: Can I refinance a fixed-rate loan?
faq.a: Yes, but you may face a break cost (sometimes called an economic cost or early termination fee). Break costs can be thousands of dollars, especially if market rates have dropped since you fixed. Ask your lender for a break cost estimate first.

faq.q: Does refinancing affect my credit score?
faq.a: Each loan application creates a hard inquiry on your credit file, which may temporarily reduce your score by a small amount. One refinancing inquiry is unlikely to have a significant long-term impact. Multiple applications in a short period can be more damaging.

howItWorks: Calculates monthly repayments for your current and proposed new loan, then compares the difference. Break-even is the number of months for cumulative savings to exceed switching costs. Total savings is the difference in total cost over the remaining loan life.

title: Do I Need to Lodge a Tax Return?
description: Answer whether you need to lodge an Australian tax return based on your income, sources, and circumstances.
metaDescription: Check if you need to lodge an Australian tax return. Answers based on your income, sources, residency, and age using ATO rules.
keywords: do I need to lodge tax return

inputs.label: Total Income for the Year
inputs.help: All income before tax — salary, investments, government payments, etc.

inputs.label: Income Sources
inputs.help: Your main income source(s)

inputs.label: Was Tax Withheld?
inputs.help: If your employer withheld tax (shown on your payment summary/income statement)

inputs.label: Had a Capital Gain?
inputs.help: Did you sell shares, property, or crypto at a profit?

faq.q: What is the tax-free threshold in Australia?
faq.a: The tax-free threshold for Australian residents is $18,200. If your total income is under this amount and no tax was withheld, you generally do not need to lodge. However, there are exceptions (e.g., if you had an ABN, capital gain, or foreign income).

faq.q: Do I need to lodge if I only earned $5,000?
faq.a: If you are an Australian resident, your income was under $18,200, no tax was withheld, and you had no other obligations (ABN, capital gains, foreign income), you generally do not need to lodge. If tax was withheld, you should lodge to get a refund.

faq.q: When is the tax return deadline?
faq.a: If lodging yourself: 31 October. If using a registered tax agent: typically extended to 15 May the following year (or later for specific categories). Late lodgement can result in penalties from the ATO.

faq.q: Do foreign residents need to lodge?
faq.a: Foreign residents must lodge if they earned any Australian-sourced income. There is no tax-free threshold for foreign residents — tax starts from the first dollar at 32.5%.

howItWorks: Applies ATO lodgement rules: checks income against the tax-free threshold ($18,200 for residents), considers whether tax was withheld (lodge to claim refund), checks for mandatory lodgement triggers (ABN income, capital gains, foreign income, government payments), and gives a yes/no answer with the specific reasoning.

title: Pregnancy Weeks Calculator
description: Track your pregnancy week, trimester, and key milestones from your due date or last menstrual period.
metaDescription: Calculate your pregnancy week and trimester from your due date or last period. See key milestones and development stages.

inputs.label: Date
inputs.help: Your due date, or the first day of your last period

faq.q: How are pregnancy weeks calculated?
faq.a: Pregnancy is counted from the first day of your last menstrual period (LMP), not from conception. This means at conception you are already considered 2 weeks pregnant. A full-term pregnancy is 40 weeks (280 days) from LMP.

faq.q: What are the trimester dates?
faq.a: First trimester: weeks 1-12. Second trimester: weeks 13-27. Third trimester: weeks 28-40. Each trimester brings different developments and symptoms.

faq.q: When should I tell my employer I am pregnant?
faq.a: Under Australian law, you must give your employer at least 10 weeks notice before your expected parental leave start date. Many women wait until after the 12-week mark when miscarriage risk decreases significantly.

faq.q: What is the paid parental leave in Australia?
faq.a: As of 2024-25, the Australian Government Paid Parental Leave scheme provides up to 22 weeks at the national minimum wage (increasing to 26 weeks by 2026). Both parents can share the leave. Check Services Australia for eligibility.

howItWorks: Calculates the number of days between today and your due date (or from your LMP). Converts to weeks and days, determines your trimester, and shows key milestones for your current stage of pregnancy.

title: Baby Age Calculator
description: Calculate your baby's exact age in weeks and months with developmental milestone markers.
metaDescription: Calculate your baby's exact age in weeks and months from their date of birth. See developmental milestones for each stage.

inputs.label: Baby's Date of Birth
inputs.help: Your baby's birthday

inputs.label: Weeks Premature (if applicable)
inputs.help: If born early, enter weeks premature for adjusted age

faq.q: What is adjusted age for premature babies?
faq.a: Adjusted (corrected) age accounts for prematurity. If a baby was born 8 weeks early and is now 20 weeks old, their adjusted age is 12 weeks. Paediatricians use adjusted age for developmental milestones until around age 2.

faq.q: When do babies start sleeping through the night?
faq.a: Most babies can sleep 6-8 hours by 3-4 months, though every baby is different. By 6 months, about 60% of babies sleep through consistently. Australian guidelines from the RCH recommend safe sleep practices from birth.

faq.q: When should my baby start solids?
faq.a: Australian dietary guidelines (NHMRC) recommend introducing solids at around 6 months, but not before 4 months. Signs of readiness include sitting upright with support, showing interest in food, and loss of the tongue-thrust reflex.

faq.q: What immunisations does my baby need?
faq.a: The Australian National Immunisation Program (NIP) provides free vaccinations at 2, 4, 6, 12, and 18 months, and at 4 years. Check the immunisation schedule at health.gov.au or your child's Blue Book.

howItWorks: Calculates the exact difference between today and the date of birth in weeks and months. If premature weeks are entered, also shows adjusted age. Maps the current age to developmental milestones from Australian child health guidelines.

title: Child Height Predictor
description: Predict your child's adult height based on mother and father's height using the mid-parental height method.
metaDescription: Predict your child's adult height from parent heights. Uses the mid-parental height formula for boys and girls.
keywords: how tall will my child be

faq.q: How accurate is the mid-parental height formula?
faq.a: The mid-parental height method predicts within +/- 10 cm (about 4 inches) in most cases. Genetics accounts for about 60-80% of height, with nutrition, health, and environment making up the rest. It is a useful estimate, not an exact prediction.

faq.q: What is the average height in Australia?
faq.a: The average adult height in Australia is approximately 175.6 cm (5'9") for men and 161.8 cm (5'4") for women, according to the ABS National Health Survey.

faq.q: At what age do children stop growing?
faq.a: Girls typically reach full adult height by age 14-16. Boys continue growing until age 16-18, sometimes up to 21. Growth plates close at different ages for different bones.

faq.q: Does nutrition affect height?
faq.a: Yes. Adequate nutrition, especially protein, calcium, vitamin D, and zinc during childhood and adolescence is important for reaching genetic height potential. Malnutrition or chronic illness during growth years can result in shorter adult height.

howItWorks: Uses the mid-parental height formula: for boys, (mother's height + father's height + 13 cm) / 2. For girls, (mother's height + father's height - 13 cm) / 2. The 13 cm represents the average height difference between adult men and women. Result has a margin of +/- 10 cm.

title: School Start Age Calculator
description: Determine when your child starts school based on their birthday and state cutoff dates across Australia.
metaDescription: Find out when your child starts school in Australia. State-by-state cutoff dates for Prep, Kindergarten, and Foundation entry.
keywords: when does my child start school

inputs.label: Child's Date of Birth
inputs.help: Your child's birthday

inputs.label: State / Territory
inputs.help: Each state has different cutoff dates and terminology

faq.q: What is the school starting age by state?
faq.a: Children typically start in the year they turn 5 or 6, depending on the state cutoff. QLD/SA/WA/NT: must turn 5 by 30 June. NSW: must turn 5 by 31 July. VIC/TAS/ACT: must turn 5 by 30 April. These are for the first year of school (Prep/Kindy/Foundation).

faq.q: What is the first year of school called?
faq.a: It varies by state: QLD = Prep, NSW = Kindergarten, VIC = Foundation (Prep), SA = Reception, WA = Pre-Primary, TAS = Prep, NT = Transition, ACT = Kindergarten. They are all equivalent to the first compulsory year of school.

faq.q: Can I hold my child back a year?
faq.a: Yes, parents can choose to delay school entry by one year. This is common for children with birthdays close to the cutoff, especially boys. Discuss with your child's preschool or the school principal for guidance.

faq.q: Is preschool compulsory in Australia?
faq.a: Preschool (the year before school) is not compulsory in most states but is strongly recommended. Queensland and NSW have made it effectively universal with funded programs. Compulsory school attendance starts from age 6 in all states.

howItWorks: Takes the child's date of birth and state, looks up the state-specific cutoff date (e.g., 30 June for QLD), and determines the first eligible school year. Shows the child's age at school entry and which calendar year they will start.

title: Sample Size Calculator
description: Calculate the required sample size for a survey or experiment given confidence level, margin of error, and population size.
metaDescription: Calculate the sample size needed for your survey or study. Set confidence level, margin of error, and population size.

inputs.label: Confidence Level
inputs.help: 95% is standard for most research

inputs.label: Margin of Error
inputs.help: +/- this percentage (5% is common)

inputs.label: Population Size
inputs.help: Total number of people in the group you are studying. Leave large (100,000+) if unknown.

inputs.label: Expected Proportion
inputs.help: Expected response rate or split. Use 50% if unknown (maximises sample).

faq.q: What sample size do I need for a 95% confidence level?
faq.a: For a 95% confidence level with +/-5% margin of error and 50% proportion: population of 1,000 needs 278 responses; 10,000 needs 370; 100,000 needs 383; 1 million+ needs 385. Larger populations barely change the required sample.

faq.q: What confidence level should I use?
faq.a: 95% is the standard for most research and surveys. 90% is acceptable for preliminary research or quick polls. 99% is used in medical, pharmaceutical, and high-stakes research where errors are costly.

faq.q: Does a larger sample always mean better results?
faq.a: Yes, up to a point. A larger sample reduces the margin of error and increases precision. However, the benefit of each additional respondent diminishes — going from 100 to 200 respondents has a bigger impact than going from 1,000 to 1,100.

faq.q: What is the margin of error?
faq.a: The margin of error represents the range within which the true population value is expected to fall. If 60% of your sample says 'yes' with a +/-5% margin, the true value is likely between 55% and 65%.

howItWorks: Uses Cochran's formula: n = (Z^2 * p * (1-p)) / E^2, adjusted for finite populations using n_adj = n / (1 + (n-1)/N). Z is the z-score for the confidence level (1.96 for 95%), p is the expected proportion, and E is the margin of error.

title: Standard Deviation Calculator
description: Calculate mean, median, mode, variance, and standard deviation from a data set.
metaDescription: Calculate standard deviation, mean, median, mode, and variance from your data set. Supports both population and sample calculations.

inputs.label: Data Set
inputs.help: Paste or type your numbers separated by commas, spaces, or new lines

inputs.label: Calculation Type
inputs.help: Use Sample for most practical purposes. Population when you have the entire data set.

faq.q: What is standard deviation?
faq.a: Standard deviation measures how spread out numbers are from the mean (average). A low SD means data points cluster close to the mean; a high SD means they are more spread out. It is the square root of the variance.

faq.q: When should I use sample vs population?
faq.a: Use population (divide by n) when your data represents the entire group. Use sample (divide by n-1) when your data is a subset — this corrects for the bias of underestimating population variance. In practice, sample is more common.

faq.q: What is a good standard deviation?
faq.a: There is no universal 'good' SD — it depends on context. In manufacturing, low SD means consistent quality. In investments, low SD means less volatility. Compare SD relative to the mean using the coefficient of variation (SD/mean x 100%).

faq.q: What is the difference between variance and standard deviation?
faq.a: Variance is the average of squared differences from the mean. Standard deviation is the square root of variance. SD is preferred for interpretation because it is in the same units as the original data.

howItWorks: Parses the input data, calculates the mean (average), then finds each value's deviation from the mean. Squares the deviations, averages them (dividing by n for population or n-1 for sample), giving variance. The square root of variance is the standard deviation. Also identifies median and mode.

title: Probability Calculator
description: Calculate combined probabilities for independent, dependent, and mutually exclusive events.
metaDescription: Calculate probability of combined events. Find P(A and B), P(A or B), and conditional probability for your scenarios.

inputs.label: P(A) — Probability of Event A
inputs.help: Enter as decimal (0.3 = 30%) or will be converted

inputs.label: P(B|A) — If Dependent
inputs.help: Probability of B given A has occurred (only needed for dependent events)

faq.q: What does independent events mean?
faq.a: Two events are independent if the outcome of one does not affect the other. For example, flipping a coin and rolling a die are independent. P(A and B) = P(A) x P(B) for independent events.

faq.q: What does mutually exclusive mean?
faq.a: Mutually exclusive events cannot happen at the same time. For example, a coin landing heads and tails on the same flip. P(A and B) = 0 and P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) for mutually exclusive events.

faq.q: How do I calculate P(A or B)?
faq.a: The addition rule: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B). For mutually exclusive events, this simplifies to P(A) + P(B) since P(A and B) = 0.

howItWorks: For independent events: P(A∩B) = P(A) x P(B) and P(A∪B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A∩B). For mutually exclusive: P(A∩B) = 0 and P(A∪B) = P(A) + P(B). For dependent events: P(A∩B) = P(A) x P(B|A). Also shows complement probabilities.

title: Scientific Notation Converter
description: Convert numbers between standard, scientific, and engineering notation.
metaDescription: Convert numbers to and from scientific notation, engineering notation, and standard form. Quick and accurate conversion tool.

inputs.label: Enter a Number
inputs.help: Enter in standard form, scientific notation (3.2e8), or with exponents

faq.q: What is scientific notation?
faq.a: Scientific notation expresses numbers as a coefficient (1-9.99) multiplied by a power of 10. For example: 300,000 = 3 x 10^5 and 0.00045 = 4.5 x 10^-4. It makes very large or very small numbers easier to read and compare.

faq.q: What is engineering notation?
faq.a: Engineering notation is similar to scientific notation but the exponent is always a multiple of 3 (corresponding to SI prefixes like kilo, mega, giga, milli, micro, nano). For example: 45,000 = 45 x 10^3.

faq.q: How do I type scientific notation?
faq.a: Most calculators and programming languages use 'e' notation: 3.2e8 means 3.2 x 10^8. Negative exponents use e-: 4.5e-4 means 4.5 x 10^-4.

howItWorks: Parses the input number (handling standard, scientific, and engineering formats), then converts to all three representations. Determines the exponent by counting decimal place shifts needed to get a coefficient between 1 and 10 (scientific) or adjusts to the nearest multiple of 3 (engineering).

title: Quadratic Equation Solver
description: Solve quadratic equations (ax^2 + bx + c = 0) for roots, discriminant, vertex, and axis of symmetry.
metaDescription: Solve any quadratic equation. Find real and complex roots, discriminant, vertex, and axis of symmetry. Shows full working.

inputs.label: a (coefficient of x²)
inputs.help: Cannot be zero

faq.q: What is the quadratic formula?
faq.a: x = (-b +/- sqrt(b^2 - 4ac)) / 2a. This formula finds the roots (x-intercepts) of any quadratic equation ax^2 + bx + c = 0.

faq.q: What does the discriminant tell you?
faq.a: The discriminant (b^2 - 4ac) determines the nature of roots: if positive, there are 2 real roots; if zero, there is 1 repeated real root; if negative, there are 2 complex conjugate roots with no real x-intercepts.

faq.q: What is the vertex of a parabola?
faq.a: The vertex is the highest or lowest point of the parabola. Its x-coordinate is -b/(2a) and the y-coordinate is found by substituting this x back into the equation. If a > 0, the vertex is the minimum; if a < 0, it is the maximum.

howItWorks: Applies the quadratic formula: x = (-b +/- sqrt(b^2 - 4ac)) / (2a). Calculates the discriminant to determine root type (real, repeated, or complex). Also computes the vertex at (-b/2a, f(-b/2a)) and the axis of symmetry at x = -b/2a.

title: Concrete Calculator
description: Calculate cubic metres of concrete needed for slabs, footings, columns, and stairs, plus bags and cost.
metaDescription: Calculate concrete volume in cubic metres for Australian projects. Covers slabs, footings, and columns with bag counts and cost estimates.
keywords: how much concrete do I need

inputs.label: Depth / Thickness

inputs.help: Allow 5-10% for waste. More for irregular shapes.

inputs.label: Cost per Cubic Metre
inputs.help: Pre-mixed concrete is typically $250-$350/m3 in Australia

faq.q: How much does concrete cost in Australia?
faq.a: Pre-mixed concrete costs approximately $250-$350 per cubic metre delivered, depending on location, mix strength, and quantity. Smaller loads (under 2m3) may incur a short-load fee of $50-$150. Pump hire is extra at $150-$300.

faq.q: How thick should a concrete slab be?
faq.a: Residential house slabs are typically 100mm (4 inches). Garage slabs are 100-150mm. Driveways should be 100-125mm. Commercial floors are 150-200mm. Reinforcement (mesh or rebar) is required for most applications.

faq.q: How many bags of concrete per cubic metre?
faq.a: For 20kg bags of premix concrete: approximately 108 bags per m3. For 40kg bags: approximately 54 bags per m3. Bag concrete is only practical for very small jobs — anything over 0.5m3 is cheaper to order pre-mixed.

faq.q: How long does concrete take to cure?
faq.a: Concrete reaches about 50% strength at 3 days, 70% at 7 days, and design strength at 28 days. You can walk on it after 24-48 hours and drive on a driveway after 7 days. Full cure takes 28 days — keep it moist during this period.

howItWorks: Calculates volume in cubic metres based on the shape dimensions (L x W x D for slabs, pi x r^2 x D for columns). Adds the waste factor percentage. Converts to number of pre-mix bags (20kg or 40kg) and estimates cost based on delivery price per cubic metre.

title: Paint Calculator
description: Calculate litres of paint needed for a room based on wall dimensions, coats, and exclusions for doors and windows.
metaDescription: Calculate how much paint you need for your room in Australia. Factors in doors, windows, coats, and coverage rate per litre.
keywords: how much paint do I need

inputs.label: Wall Height
inputs.help: Standard Australian ceiling height is 2.4m or 2.7m

inputs.label: Number of Coats
inputs.help: 2 coats is standard. Dark colours over light may need 3.

inputs.label: Coverage Rate
inputs.help: Typical wall paint covers 10-14 m² per litre

inputs.label: Paint Cost (per litre)
inputs.help: Dulux Wash & Wear is ~$15/L in 15L buckets

faq.q: How much paint per square metre?
faq.a: Standard wall paint covers 10-14 m² per litre for one coat, depending on surface texture and paint quality. Rough surfaces and porous plaster need more paint. Always check the specific product's coverage rate on the tin.

faq.q: How much does a room cost to paint in Australia?
faq.a: DIY paint for a standard bedroom (4x3m) costs about $100-$200 in materials (paint, tape, drop sheets). Professional painters charge $250-$600 per room depending on preparation, size, and location.

faq.q: Do I need primer?
faq.a: Primer is recommended for new plasterboard, bare timber, stained surfaces, and when making a dramatic colour change. Self-priming paints (like Dulux Wash & Wear) can skip primer on previously painted surfaces in good condition.

faq.q: What size tins should I buy?
faq.a: Paint is available in 1L, 2L, 4L, 10L, and 15L. The best value per litre is 10L or 15L tins. Buy the smallest size that covers your needs to avoid waste, but round up — leftover paint is useful for touch-ups.

howItWorks: Calculates total wall area (perimeter x height), subtracts door (1.8m²) and window (1.5m²) areas, optionally adds ceiling area (length x width), multiplies by number of coats, then divides by coverage rate to get litres needed. Rounds up to practical tin sizes.

title: Timber Calculator
description: Calculate timber pieces, lengths, and cost for framing, decking, fencing, and other projects with waste factor.
metaDescription: Calculate timber quantities for Australian building projects. Covers framing, decking, and fencing with waste factor and cost estimate.
keywords: how much timber do I need

inputs.label: Total Length / Run
inputs.help: Total wall length, floor length, or fence length

inputs.label: Height / Width
inputs.help: Wall height, floor width, or joist span

inputs.label: Spacing (centres)
inputs.help: Stud/joist spacing. Standard: 450mm or 600mm centres.

inputs.label: Waste Factor
inputs.help: Allow 10% for cuts and waste

inputs.label: Price per Lineal Metre
inputs.help: Check Bunnings or your timber supplier for current prices

faq.q: What spacing for wall studs in Australia?
faq.a: Standard stud spacing is 450mm or 600mm centres under the National Construction Code (NCC). 450mm is used for most residential walls. 600mm is acceptable for non-load-bearing internal walls and where sheet cladding allows it.

faq.q: What size timber for wall framing?
faq.a: Standard residential wall framing uses 90x45mm (or 70x45mm for non-load-bearing). Top and bottom plates are the same size. For load-bearing walls or two-storey construction, 90x45mm or larger is typically specified by the engineer.

faq.q: How much does framing timber cost in Australia?
faq.a: Treated pine framing (90x45mm) costs approximately $4-$7 per lineal metre. Prices vary by region, supplier, and treatment level. Bulk orders from timber yards are cheaper than retail (Bunnings).

faq.q: What timber treatment do I need?
faq.a: H2 for internal framing protected from weather. H3 for outdoor above-ground (decks, fences). H4 for in-ground contact (posts, retaining walls). H5 for in-ground in high-risk areas. H6 for marine use.

howItWorks: Calculates the number of pieces based on total length divided by spacing, plus one (e.g., a 10m wall at 450mm centres needs 23 studs). Multiplies by the piece length (wall height or span). Adds the waste factor and multiplies by cost per lineal metre for the total estimate.

title: Roofing Calculator
description: Calculate roofing materials (sheets or tiles), accessories, and cost based on roof area, pitch, and material type.
metaDescription: Calculate roofing materials for Australian homes. Covers Colorbond sheets and concrete tiles with accessories and cost estimate.

inputs.label: Roof Plan Area
inputs.help: Flat plan area of the roof (footprint)

inputs.label: Roof Pitch
inputs.help: Steeper pitch = more actual surface area

inputs.label: Cost per Sheet/Tile
inputs.help: Colorbond sheets ~$20-35 each. Concrete tiles ~$1.50-2.50 each.

faq.q: How many Colorbond sheets per square metre?
faq.a: Standard Colorbond corrugated sheets (762mm cover width) require approximately 1.3 sheets per m² of actual roof area. Custom Orb and Trimdek have different cover widths — check the BlueScope specification for your profile.

faq.q: How many roof tiles per square metre?
faq.a: Concrete tiles: approximately 9-10 tiles per m². Terracotta tiles: approximately 11-13 per m². This varies by tile profile and overlap. Always check the manufacturer's specification.

faq.q: What is the cheapest roofing material in Australia?
faq.a: Colorbond steel is typically the most cost-effective for new builds at $50-$80/m² installed. Concrete tiles are $60-$90/m² installed. Terracotta tiles are $80-$130/m² installed. Metal is lighter and faster to install.

faq.q: How long does a Colorbond roof last?
faq.a: BlueScope offers a 25-year warranty on Colorbond steel. In practice, Colorbond roofs in Australia commonly last 40-60 years with minimal maintenance. Coastal and industrial environments may reduce lifespan.

howItWorks: Adjusts the plan area to actual roof surface area using the pitch factor (plan area / cos(pitch angle)). Divides by the coverage area per sheet or tile to get the quantity. Adds the waste factor and calculates accessory requirements (ridge caps, screws, sarking).

title: Fencing Calculator
description: Calculate materials (posts, rails, palings/panels) and cost for timber, Colorbond, or pool fencing.
metaDescription: Calculate fencing materials and cost for Australian properties. Covers timber, Colorbond, and pool fencing with full materials list.

inputs.label: Fence Height

inputs.label: Post Spacing

faq.q: How much does Colorbond fencing cost in Australia?
faq.a: Colorbond fencing costs approximately $100-$150 per lineal metre installed (1800mm height). DIY materials only cost $65-$90/m. Prices vary by region — metro areas are typically cheaper than rural due to competition and transport.

faq.q: What are the fencing laws in Australia?
faq.a: Boundary fences are generally shared responsibility (50/50) between neighbours under each state's fencing act. Height limits vary by council but standard residential is 1.8m (rear/side) and 1.2m (front). Always check with your local council.

faq.q: What depth should fence posts be?
faq.a: Fence posts should be set at least 600mm deep (a third of the total post length). In soft or sandy soil, go deeper (750-900mm). Posts should be set in concrete (1-2 bags per post). End and corner posts may need bracing.

faq.q: What is the minimum pool fence height?
faq.a: Under AS 1926.1, pool fencing must be at least 1200mm high with no climbable objects within 900mm. Gates must be self-closing and self-latching, opening outward from the pool. Non-climbable zone (NCZ) requirements apply to all pool barriers.

howItWorks: Calculates the number of posts (length / spacing + 1), rails (typically 2-3 per bay), and palings or panels per bay. Includes gate hardware and concrete for post footings. Multiplies by unit costs for a materials breakdown, or uses cost-per-metre for a quick total estimate.

faq.q: How much does a deck cost in Australia?
faq.a: DIY materials for a 15m² merbau deck cost approximately $2,500-$4,000. Professionally built: $350-$600/m² for hardwood, $300-$500/m² for composite, $200-$350/m² for treated pine. These prices include subframe, boards, and basic stairs.

faq.q: Do I need a permit to build a deck?
faq.a: In most Australian states, decks under 1m high and under a certain area (10m² in some states, 20m² in others) are exempt from building approval. Anything higher or larger usually needs a building permit. Always check with your local council.

faq.q: What is the best decking timber in Australia?
faq.a: Merbau is the most popular for its durability, colour, and value. Spotted gum and ironbark are premium Australian hardwoods. Treated pine is budget-friendly. Composite decking (like Modwood) is low-maintenance but costs more upfront.

faq.q: How long does merbau decking last?
faq.a: Properly maintained merbau decking lasts 25-40 years. Oil it every 12-18 months with a quality decking oil. It will bleed tannins (red staining) for the first few months — this is normal and washes away over time.

howItWorks: Calculates the number of boards (deck width / (board width + gap)), each at the deck length. Calculates joists (deck length / joist spacing + 1) each at the deck width. Adds waste factor, then estimates fixings (approximately 20-25 screws per m²). Totals all material costs.

title: Blood Alcohol Calculator
description: Estimate your blood alcohol concentration (BAC) based on drinks, weight, gender, and time, with time to reach 0.05 and 0.00.
metaDescription: Estimate your BAC and time to reach 0.05 or 0.00 in Australia. Based on drinks, weight, and time since drinking.

inputs.label: Number of Standard Drinks
inputs.help: 1 standard drink = 10g alcohol (a middy of beer, small glass of wine, or 30ml spirit)

faq.q: What is the legal BAC limit in Australia?
faq.a: The legal limit for full licence holders in all states is 0.05 BAC. Learner and provisional drivers must have 0.00 BAC. Professional drivers (taxi, bus, truck) have a 0.00 limit. Penalties vary by state but include fines, licence suspension, and potential imprisonment.

faq.q: What is a standard drink in Australia?
faq.a: One standard drink contains 10 grams of alcohol. Examples: a 285ml glass (middy/pot) of full-strength beer, a 100ml glass of wine (13%), a 30ml shot of spirits (40%). Check the label — standard drinks are marked on all Australian alcohol products.

faq.q: How long does it take to process one standard drink?
faq.a: Your body processes approximately one standard drink per hour. This rate is relatively constant regardless of weight or fitness. Coffee, cold showers, and food do not speed up alcohol processing — only time works.

faq.q: Is this calculator legally accurate?
faq.a: No BAC calculator can be 100% accurate. Actual BAC depends on many factors including metabolism, food intake, medications, liver function, and individual variation. This is an estimate only. If in doubt, do not drive. Use a personal breathalyser or wait longer.

howItWorks: Uses the Widmark formula: BAC = (drinks x 10 / (weight x gender factor)) - (hours x 0.015). Gender factor is 0.68 for males and 0.55 for females. The elimination rate of 0.015 per hour represents average liver processing. Also calculates time to reach 0.05 and 0.00 BAC.

title: Electricity Usage Cost Calculator
description: Calculate the running cost of any appliance based on its wattage, usage hours, and your electricity tariff.
metaDescription: Calculate how much any appliance costs to run in Australia. Enter wattage, hours, and your electricity rate to see daily and annual cost.
keywords: how much does it cost to run

inputs.label: Appliance Wattage
inputs.help: Check the appliance label or manual for wattage

inputs.label: Electricity Rate
inputs.help: Check your bill. Average Australian rate is ~30c/kWh.

faq.q: How much does a heater cost to run?
faq.a: A 2400W electric heater running 4 hours per day at 30c/kWh costs about $2.88/day or $520/year over winter (6 months). A reverse-cycle air conditioner is 3-5x more efficient — a 2.5kW unit costs about $0.60-$1.00/day.

faq.q: What appliances use the most electricity?
faq.a: Biggest energy users in Australian homes: ducted air conditioning (30-40%), electric hot water (20-25%), pool pump (10-15%), clothes dryer (5-10%), and refrigerator (5-8%). Switching to gas or solar hot water and efficient AC makes the biggest impact.

faq.q: How do I find my appliance wattage?
faq.a: Check the nameplate label on the appliance (usually on the back or bottom), the user manual, or the manufacturer's website. Common wattages: kettle 2000-2400W, microwave 600-1200W, TV 100-300W, fridge 100-400W, laptop 30-90W.

faq.q: What is a kWh?
faq.a: A kilowatt-hour (kWh) is the energy used by a 1000W appliance running for 1 hour. Your electricity bill charges per kWh consumed. A 2400W heater running for 1 hour uses 2.4 kWh.

howItWorks: Converts wattage to kilowatts (W/1000), multiplies by hours per day to get daily kWh consumption. Multiplies by the electricity tariff to get daily cost. Extrapolates to weekly, monthly, quarterly, and annual costs based on days per week of usage.

title: Solar Savings Calculator
description: Estimate solar panel savings, payback period, and return on investment based on your state, system size, and tariffs.
metaDescription: Calculate solar panel savings in Australia by state. See payback period, annual savings, and ROI for your system size and tariff.

inputs.label: State / Territory
inputs.help: Solar production varies significantly by state and location

inputs.label: System Size
inputs.help: 6.6kW is the most popular residential size in Australia

inputs.label: Electricity Tariff
inputs.help: What you pay per kWh from the grid

inputs.label: Feed-In Tariff
inputs.help: What you receive for exporting solar to the grid

inputs.label: Self-Consumption Rate
inputs.help: % of solar used at home (30% typical without battery, 60-80% with battery)

inputs.label: System Cost (after rebate)
inputs.help: After STC rebate. 6.6kW systems typically $4,500-$7,000 installed.

faq.q: How much does a 6.6kW solar system cost in Australia?
faq.a: After the STC rebate (federal Small-scale Technology Certificate), a quality 6.6kW system costs $4,500-$7,000 installed in 2024-25. Prices vary by brand, installer, and location. Budget systems can be cheaper but may use lower-quality components.

faq.q: How much does solar save per year?
faq.a: A 6.6kW system in Brisbane saves approximately $1,400-$1,800/year depending on self-consumption and tariffs. In Melbourne, savings are $1,100-$1,500/year due to less sunlight. Higher self-consumption (using solar during the day) maximises savings.

faq.q: Is a solar battery worth it in Australia?
faq.a: At current prices ($8,000-$15,000 for 10-13kWh), batteries have a payback of 10-15 years, which is close to their warranty period. Batteries make more financial sense if you have time-of-use tariffs, high evening usage, or want blackout protection.

faq.q: What is the STC rebate?
faq.a: The Small-scale Technology Certificate (STC) scheme provides an upfront rebate on solar installations. For a 6.6kW system in Brisbane (2024), the rebate is approximately $2,500-$3,000. The rebate decreases each year until 2030 when the scheme ends.

howItWorks: Estimates daily solar production based on system size, state irradiance data, and roof direction factor. Splits production into self-consumed (valued at usage tariff) and exported (valued at feed-in tariff). Calculates annual savings and divides system cost by annual savings for payback period.

title: Water Usage Calculator
description: Estimate your household water consumption in litres per day and quarterly cost based on usage habits.
metaDescription: Estimate your Australian household water usage and cost. Track shower, laundry, garden, and appliance consumption in litres per day.

inputs.label: Average Shower Length
inputs.help: Average Australian shower is 7 minutes

inputs.label: Garden Watering (min/week)
inputs.help: Total minutes per week using hose or sprinkler

inputs.label: Water Rate
inputs.help: Cost per kilolitre (1000 litres). Check your water bill.

faq.q: What is the average water usage in Australia?
faq.a: The average Australian uses approximately 340 litres per person per day (including outdoor use). Indoor use averages about 200 litres/day. The largest indoor uses are shower (34%), toilet (26%), laundry (23%), and kitchen (13%).

faq.q: How much does water cost in Australia?
faq.a: Water prices vary by utility. Usage charges range from $2.00-$4.50 per kilolitre (1000 litres) plus a fixed service charge of $100-$300/quarter. Sydney Water charges ~$2.35/kL. Urban Utilities (Brisbane) charges ~$3.20/kL.

faq.q: How much water does a 10-minute shower use?
faq.a: With a standard 9 L/min shower head: 90 litres. With an old 15 L/min head: 150 litres. With a water-efficient 6 L/min head: 60 litres. Replacing an old shower head is one of the easiest water-saving upgrades.

faq.q: Are there water restrictions in my area?
faq.a: Water restrictions vary by local council and dam levels. Most Australian cities have permanent water-saving rules (e.g., no hosing driveways, watering gardens only on certain days/times). Check your local water authority's website for current restrictions.

howItWorks: Estimates daily water consumption by category: showers (flow rate x minutes x showers/day), toilet flushes (~35L/person/day), washing machine (~60-120L per load), dishwasher (~15L/load), kitchen/drinking (~20L/person/day), and garden watering. Converts to quarterly cost using the water rate.

title: Moving Cost Estimator
description: Estimate the cost of moving house in Australia based on distance, bedrooms, floor access, and packing needs.
metaDescription: Estimate your Australian moving cost. Based on bedrooms, distance, floor access, and packing requirements for local and interstate moves.

faq.q: How much does a removalist cost in Australia?
faq.a: Local moves: 1-bed ~$300-$600, 2-bed ~$500-$900, 3-bed ~$700-$1,500, 4-bed ~$1,000-$2,000. Interstate (e.g., Sydney to Brisbane): 3-bed ~$3,000-$6,000. Prices depend on access, packing, volume, and time of year (Jan-Feb is peak).

faq.q: Should I hire a removalist or use a trailer?
faq.a: For a small 1-bed apartment with no heavy furniture, a trailer hire ($50-$100) can save money. For anything larger, a removalist is usually more cost-effective when you factor in time, risk of damage, and the physical effort. Get at least 3 quotes.

faq.q: What is the cheapest day to move?
faq.a: Mid-week (Tuesday-Thursday) and mid-month are cheapest. Avoid end of month (lease cycles), weekends, public holidays, and January-February (peak moving season). Some removalists offer 10-20% discounts for mid-week moves.

faq.q: Does insurance cover my belongings during a move?
faq.a: Standard home insurance usually does not cover items during transit. Most reputable removalists offer transit insurance ($30-$100 depending on value). Always check the policy details — some only cover total loss, not individual item damage.

howItWorks: Estimates moving cost based on a baseline rate for bedroom count and distance, then adjusts for floor access surcharges (stairs add $100-$300), packing service (partial adds 15-25%, full adds 30-50%), and distance. Shows a low-to-high cost range reflecting market variation.

title: Pet Age Calculator
description: Convert your pet's age to human-equivalent years based on species and breed size.
metaDescription: Calculate your pet's age in human years. Covers dogs by breed size, cats, and other pets with accurate age conversion.
keywords: how old is my dog in human years

inputs.label: Breed Size (dogs only)
inputs.help: Larger dogs age faster than smaller dogs

faq.q: Is 1 dog year really 7 human years?
faq.a: No, the 1:7 ratio is a myth. Dogs age much faster in their first two years (a 2-year-old dog is roughly equivalent to a 24-year-old human). After that, each year equals about 4-5 human years, varying by breed size — small dogs age slower than large dogs.

faq.q: How long do dogs live in Australia?
faq.a: Average lifespan varies by breed: small breeds (Chihuahua, Maltese) 12-16 years, medium breeds (Border Collie, Staffy) 10-14 years, large breeds (Labrador, Golden Retriever) 10-12 years, giant breeds (Great Dane, Mastiff) 7-10 years.

faq.q: How old is my cat in human years?
faq.a: Cats reach the equivalent of about 24 human years by age 2. After that, each cat year equals roughly 4 human years. So a 10-year-old cat is about 56 in human years. Indoor cats typically live 15-20 years; outdoor cats 10-15 years.

faq.q: When is a dog considered a senior?
faq.a: Small breeds: 10-12 years. Medium breeds: 8-10 years. Large breeds: 6-8 years. Giant breeds: 5-6 years. Senior pets benefit from more frequent vet check-ups (every 6 months) and age-appropriate nutrition.

howItWorks: Uses breed-specific ageing curves rather than a simple multiplier. For dogs, the first year equals 15 human years, the second year adds 9, and subsequent years add 4-7 depending on breed size. Cats follow a similar non-linear curve. The result is an approximate human-equivalent age.

title: Shoe Size Converter
description: Convert shoe sizes between Australian, US, UK, and EU systems for men, women, and children.
metaDescription: Convert shoe sizes between Australian, US, UK, and EU systems. Covers men's, women's, and children's sizes with foot length guide.

inputs.label: Foot Length
inputs.help: Measure from heel to longest toe while standing

faq.q: Are Australian shoe sizes the same as US?
faq.a: For men's shoes, Australian sizes are generally the same as US sizes. For women's shoes, AU sizes are 2 sizes smaller than US (e.g., AU 8 = US 10). For children's shoes, AU and US sizes are the same.

faq.q: How do I measure my foot correctly?
faq.a: Stand on a piece of paper with your heel against a wall. Mark the end of your longest toe. Measure from the wall to the mark in centimetres. Measure both feet — use the larger measurement. Measure at the end of the day when feet are largest.

faq.q: What is a UK vs AU shoe size?
faq.a: For men, AU sizes are typically 0.5-1 size larger than UK. For women, AU and UK sizes are usually the same. EU sizes are different — an AU men's 9 is approximately EU 43.

howItWorks: Takes the foot length in centimetres and applies the standard conversion formulas for each sizing system. AU/US men's are closely aligned. Women's AU is offset from US by 2 sizes. EU uses the Paris point system (foot length cm x 1.5 + 2). Displays all systems side by side.

title: Dress Size Converter
description: Convert dress sizes between Australian, US, UK, and EU sizing systems.
metaDescription: Convert dress sizes between Australian, US, UK, and EU systems. Quick reference for online shopping across international brands.

faq.q: How does AU dress sizing compare to US?
faq.a: Australian dress sizes are typically 4 sizes larger in number than US sizes. An AU 12 is a US 8. An AU 10 is a US 6. However, sizing varies between brands — always check the specific brand's size chart.

faq.q: What is the average dress size in Australia?
faq.a: The average Australian woman wears a size 14-16. The fashion industry standard size in Australia has been shifting, with many brands now offering extended size ranges (6-26 or larger).

faq.q: Are UK and AU sizes the same?
faq.a: Almost. AU and UK dress sizes are very similar. An AU 12 is roughly a UK 12, though some brands show a 2-size difference (AU 12 = UK 14). Always check the specific brand's conversion chart.

howItWorks: Applies standard dress size conversion offsets between sizing systems. AU to US: subtract 4. AU to UK: same or +2 depending on brand. AU to EU: add 28 (AU 12 = EU 40). Displays all systems in a conversion table for quick reference.

title: Bra Size Calculator
description: Calculate your Australian bra size from under-bust and over-bust measurements, with sister sizes.
metaDescription: Calculate your Australian bra size from body measurements. Includes sister sizes and international size conversion.

inputs.label: Under-Bust Measurement
inputs.help: Measure snugly around your ribcage directly under your bust

inputs.label: Over-Bust Measurement
inputs.help: Measure around the fullest part of your bust without compressing

faq.q: How do I measure for a bra correctly?
faq.a: Use a soft tape measure. Under-bust: measure around your ribcage directly under your bust, keeping the tape snug but not tight. Over-bust: measure around the fullest part of your bust, keeping the tape level and not compressing. Measure while wearing a non-padded bra.

faq.q: What are sister sizes?
faq.a: Sister sizes have the same cup volume but different band sizes. If a 12C is slightly tight in the band, try a 14B (same cup volume, larger band). If it is loose, try a 10D (same cup volume, smaller band).

faq.q: What is the most common bra size in Australia?
faq.a: The most commonly sold bra size in Australia is reportedly 14C-D. However, research suggests many women wear the wrong size — studies indicate up to 80% of women may be wearing an incorrect bra size.

faq.q: How do AU bra sizes compare to US?
faq.a: AU band sizes are 16 numbers larger than US (AU 12 = US 34). Cup letters are generally the same (AU C = US C). So AU 12C = US 34C. UK sizing is similar to AU for cups but uses different band numbers.

howItWorks: Rounds the under-bust measurement to the nearest even number for the band size. Calculates the difference between over-bust and under-bust measurements — each 2cm difference equals one cup size (A=10cm, B=12cm, C=14cm, D=16cm, DD=18cm, E=20cm, F=22cm). Also generates sister sizes one band up and down.

title: Rental Yield Calculator
description: Calculate gross and net rental yield, plus annual cashflow for an investment property.
metaDescription: Calculate gross and net rental yield for Australian investment property. See annual cashflow after expenses and vacancy.

inputs.label: Vacancy Rate
inputs.help: Average Australian vacancy rate is 1-3%

inputs.label: Property Management Fee
inputs.help: Typically 5-8% of rent. 0% if self-managed.

inputs.label: Annual Expenses
inputs.help: Council rates, insurance, strata, maintenance, water

faq.q: What is a good rental yield in Australia?
faq.a: Gross yields of 4-6% are considered good for capital city properties. Regional areas can achieve 6-10%+. Net yield (after expenses) is typically 1-3% lower. Higher yields often come with lower capital growth potential and vice versa.

faq.q: How do you calculate rental yield?
faq.a: Gross yield = (annual rent / property value) x 100. Net yield = ((annual rent - annual expenses) / property value) x 100. For example, $550/week rent on a $600,000 property = $28,600 / $600,000 = 4.77% gross yield.

faq.q: What expenses can I claim on an investment property?
faq.a: Deductible expenses include: loan interest, property management fees, insurance, council rates, water rates, strata fees, repairs and maintenance, depreciation (building and fixtures), advertising for tenants, and travel to inspect the property (if meeting ATO rules).

faq.q: What vacancy rate should I use?
faq.a: National average vacancy is 1-2% (2024). Use 2-3% for capital cities, 3-5% for regional towns. High-demand inner-city apartments may have near-zero vacancy. Always check SQM Research or Domain for current local vacancy rates.

howItWorks: Calculates gross yield from annual rent (weekly x 52) divided by property value. Adjusts for vacancy rate, then subtracts management fees and annual expenses for net yield. Cashflow is annual rent minus vacancy, management, and all expenses — positive means the property covers its costs before loan repayments.

title: Negative Gearing Calculator
description: Calculate the tax benefit of negative gearing on an Australian investment property.
metaDescription: Calculate the Australian negative gearing tax benefit on your investment property. See the loss, tax offset, and after-tax cost of holding.

inputs.label: Annual Expenses
inputs.help: Rates, insurance, strata, management, maintenance, depreciation

inputs.label: Marginal Tax Rate
inputs.help: Higher tax rate = bigger tax benefit from negative gearing

faq.q: What is negative gearing?
faq.a: Negative gearing occurs when the costs of owning an investment property (loan interest, expenses) exceed the rental income, creating a loss. This loss can be offset against your other income (e.g., salary), reducing your taxable income and therefore your tax.

faq.q: How much does negative gearing save on tax?
faq.a: The tax saving equals the net loss multiplied by your marginal tax rate. For example, a $10,000 annual loss at a 37% tax rate saves $3,700 in tax. The higher your marginal rate, the bigger the benefit — this is why negative gearing favours higher-income earners.

faq.q: Is negative gearing still worth it?
faq.a: Negative gearing only makes sense if you expect capital growth to exceed the after-tax cost of holding. A property that costs you $200/week after tax but grows 5% ($30,000/year on $600,000) is profitable. But if prices stagnate, you are just losing money with a tax discount.

faq.q: Can I negatively gear shares?
faq.a: Yes. Negative gearing applies to any income-producing investment, not just property. If you borrow to buy shares and the interest exceeds dividends received, the loss is deductible against your other income.

howItWorks: Calculates total annual costs (loan interest + expenses) minus annual rental income to determine the net loss. Multiplies the loss by your marginal tax rate to find the tax benefit. Shows the after-tax cost of holding the property (loss minus tax benefit) — this is your real out-of-pocket cost.

title: Dividend Reinvestment Calculator
description: Project investment growth with and without dividend reinvestment (DRP) over time.
metaDescription: Calculate the power of dividend reinvestment (DRP) on Australian shares. Compare growth with and without reinvesting dividends.

inputs.label: Dividend Yield
inputs.help: Current dividend yield. ASX average is ~4%

inputs.label: Annual Capital Growth
inputs.help: Expected annual share price growth

inputs.label: Annual Additional Investment
inputs.help: Extra amount invested each year (on top of DRP)

faq.q: What is the average dividend yield on the ASX?
faq.a: The ASX 200 dividend yield averages around 4-4.5% including franking credits. High-yield stocks (banks, miners) can yield 5-7%+. REITs (A-REITs) yield 4-6%. Growth stocks may yield 1-2% or pay no dividends.

faq.q: What are franking credits?
faq.a: Franking credits (imputation credits) represent tax already paid by the company on its profits. A fully franked dividend of $70 carries $30 in franking credits (at 30% company tax rate), making the gross dividend $100. You declare $100 as income and claim $30 as a tax offset.

faq.q: How much difference does DRP make?
faq.a: Over 20 years, reinvesting a 4.5% dividend on $20,000 (with 5% capital growth and no additional investment) grows to approximately $93,000 with DRP vs $73,000 without. That is a 27% difference from compounding alone. The gap widens dramatically over longer periods.

faq.q: Should I use DRP or buy shares myself?
faq.a: DRP is automatic and usually free of brokerage. However, buying manually lets you choose timing and price, and you can invest in different stocks. Some DRP plans offer a small discount (1-5%) on the share price.

howItWorks: Projects investment growth year by year. Capital growth increases the portfolio value. Dividends are calculated on the current value. With DRP on, dividends are added to the investment (compounding). With DRP off, dividends are paid as cash and only capital growth compounds. Additional annual investments are added each year.

title: Crypto Profit & Loss Calculator
description: Calculate cryptocurrency profit, ROI, and estimated Australian CGT on your trades.
metaDescription: Calculate crypto profit, loss, ROI, and Australian capital gains tax. Covers Bitcoin, Ethereum, and all cryptocurrencies.

inputs.label: Quantity
inputs.help: Number of coins or tokens

inputs.label: Buy Fee
inputs.help: Exchange fee when buying (Coinspot ~1%, Binance ~0.1%)

inputs.label: Holding Period
inputs.help: 50% CGT discount applies if held 12+ months

faq.q: How is crypto taxed in Australia?
faq.a: The ATO treats crypto as a CGT asset. Every disposal (selling, trading, swapping, or spending crypto) is a CGT event. Profits are added to your taxable income. Losses can be offset against crypto gains but not other income. The 50% CGT discount applies if held 12+ months.

faq.q: Do I pay tax on crypto I have not sold?
faq.a: No. Simply holding cryptocurrency does not trigger a tax event. You only pay tax when you dispose of it (sell, trade, swap, spend, or gift). Transferring between your own wallets is not a taxable event.

faq.q: What records do I need for crypto tax?
faq.a: The ATO requires records of: date of each transaction, the amount in AUD at the time, what the transaction was for, the other party's wallet address (from exchange records). Most exchanges provide transaction history. Use a crypto tax tool like Koinly or CryptoTaxCalculator for complex portfolios.

faq.q: Is crypto-to-crypto trading taxable?
faq.a: Yes. Swapping one cryptocurrency for another (e.g., BTC to ETH) is a CGT event in Australia. You must calculate the gain or loss in AUD at the time of the swap. This catches many people unaware.

howItWorks: Calculates total cost (buy price x quantity + buy fee) and total proceeds (sell price x quantity - sell fee). Profit is proceeds minus cost. ROI is profit divided by cost as a percentage. Estimated CGT applies the 50% discount if held 12+ months, then taxes at your marginal rate.

title: Love Calculator
description: Enter two names and get a fun compatibility percentage score. For entertainment only!
metaDescription: Fun love compatibility calculator. Enter two names to see your match percentage. Purely for entertainment — no relationship advice!

faq.q: How does the love calculator work?
faq.a: It uses a fun algorithm based on the letters in both names to generate a percentage. It is purely for entertainment — no scientific basis whatsoever. Do not make relationship decisions based on this!

faq.q: Is this calculator accurate?
faq.a: No. This is a fun novelty calculator for entertainment purposes only. Real compatibility depends on communication, shared values, mutual respect, and effort — not a name algorithm.

faq.q: Why do I get different scores with different names?
faq.a: The algorithm processes the combined letters of both names using a consistent hash function, so different letter combinations produce different scores. Nicknames vs full names will give different results.

howItWorks: Combines both names, counts the occurrences of letters in the word 'LOVES' and 'TRUE', then applies a deterministic algorithm to produce a percentage between 1-99%. The same two names always produce the same result. This is entertainment only.

title: Pizza Split Calculator
description: Calculate how to fairly split pizza based on size, number of pizzas, people, and hunger levels.
metaDescription: Calculate fair pizza splits. How many slices per person based on pizza size, number of pizzas, and group size. With area per person!

inputs.label: Slices Per Pizza
inputs.help: Standard: 8 for large, 6 for small

faq.q: How many pizzas for 10 people?
faq.a: Rule of thumb: 3 slices per adult (light eaters) to 4-5 slices (hungry adults). For 10 people with large pizzas (8 slices each): 4 pizzas for light eating, 5-6 for hungry crowds. Add 1 extra for safety.

faq.q: Is two medium pizzas bigger than one large?
faq.a: No! This is the famous pizza paradox. An 18-inch pizza has about 254 square inches of pizza. Two 12-inch pizzas have about 226 square inches total. One larger pizza gives you more pizza than two smaller ones because area scales with the square of the diameter.

faq.q: How much does pizza delivery cost in Australia?
faq.a: Domino's and Pizza Hut charge $8-$18 per pizza (with frequent deals). Gourmet pizzerias charge $18-$30. Delivery fees are typically $5-$10. Value picks and bundle deals are usually the best bang for your buck.

howItWorks: Calculates total pizza area using pi x r^2 for each pizza, sums them, then divides by number of people for area per person. Also calculates total slices and slices per person. Provides a fun comparison of pizza area to common objects.

title: Coffee Cost Calculator
description: Calculate how much you spend on takeaway coffee per year, over 5, 10, and 30 years — and what it could grow to if invested.
metaDescription: Calculate your annual takeaway coffee spend in Australia and what it could be worth invested over 5, 10, and 30 years.
keywords: how much do I spend on coffee

inputs.label: Price Per Coffee
inputs.help: Average flat white in Australia is $5-$6

inputs.label: Days Per Week
inputs.help: Work days only, or every day?

inputs.label: Investment Return (if saved)
inputs.help: What this money could earn if invested instead

faq.q: How much does the average Australian spend on coffee?
faq.a: Australians spend an estimated $4-$8 billion on coffee per year. The average daily coffee drinker spends approximately $1,500-$2,800 per year on takeaway coffee ($5-$6 per cup, 5-7 days a week).

faq.q: What is the latte factor?
faq.a: The 'latte factor' is a personal finance concept showing how small daily expenses add up over time. A $5.50 coffee every workday costs $1,430/year. Invested at 7% for 30 years, that annual amount grows to approximately $145,000.

faq.q: How much does it cost to make coffee at home?
faq.a: Home-brewed coffee costs $0.20-$0.80 per cup depending on method. Pods: $0.50-$0.80. Espresso machine with beans: $0.30-$0.50. Filter/plunger: $0.15-$0.30. Instant: $0.10-$0.20. Even a high-end home setup saves $3-$5 per cup vs takeaway.

faq.q: Should I stop buying coffee to save money?
faq.a: Not necessarily — life is about balance. But knowing the true cost helps you make informed choices. Maybe switch to home-brew on some days, or redirect the savings to a goal that motivates you. A $5.50 daily saving adds up to $25,000+ over 10 years.

howItWorks: Multiplies coffees per day by price per coffee by days per week by 52 weeks for annual cost. Projects the opportunity cost of that money if invested using compound interest over 5, 10, 20, and 30 year horizons. Also compares to the cost of making coffee at home.

title: Property Buying Costs Calculator
description: Estimate the full cost of buying a property in Australia, including stamp duty by state, legal fees, building and pest inspections, LMI, and total funds required.
metaDescription: Calculate all the costs of buying property in Australia — stamp duty, legal fees, LMI, inspections and more. Updated 2024-25 rates for all states.

inputs.label: Property Price
inputs.help: The purchase price of the property

faq.q: How much does it cost to buy a house in Australia beyond the purchase price?
faq.a: On top of the purchase price you typically pay stamp duty, legal and conveyancing fees ($1,000–$2,500), building and pest inspections (~$800), a loan application fee (~$400), and potentially Lenders Mortgage Insurance (LMI) if your deposit is under 20%. Total buying costs commonly range from 3% to 6% of the purchase price.

faq.q: What is stamp duty and how is it calculated?
faq.a: Stamp duty (transfer duty) is a state-based tax on property transactions. Each state has its own sliding-scale rates. For example, a $700,000 purchase in NSW attracts approximately $26,730 in stamp duty. First home buyers may be eligible for concessions or exemptions.

faq.q: What is LMI and when do I have to pay it?
faq.a: Lenders Mortgage Insurance (LMI) is a premium you pay when your deposit is less than 20% of the purchase price (i.e. LVR above 80%). LMI protects the lender — not you — against default. On a $700,000 property with a 10% deposit, LMI can be $8,000–$18,000.

faq.q: Do first home buyers pay stamp duty?
faq.a: It depends on the state and the purchase price. NSW first home buyers pay no stamp duty on properties under $800,000 (established) or $1M (new). Victorian first home buyers pay no duty under $600,000. ACT first home buyers pay no duty under $900,000. Other states have varying thresholds.

faq.q: Can I include buying costs in my home loan?
faq.a: In some cases yes — some lenders allow you to capitalise costs like LMI into the loan. However, stamp duty and legal fees generally need to be paid from your own savings. Always confirm with your lender.

faq.q: How much does it cost to sell a house in Australia?
faq.a: Selling costs typically total 2%–4% of the sale price. The biggest cost is agent commission (1.5%–3.5%), followed by marketing ($3,000–$10,000), conveyancing (~$1,100), auctioneer fees (~$600), and a mortgage discharge fee (~$400) if applicable.

faq.q: Do I pay capital gains tax when I sell my home?
faq.a: If the property was your principal place of residence for the entire ownership period, you are generally exempt from CGT. If it was an investment property or rented out at any point, CGT will apply. Properties held over 12 months benefit from a 50% CGT discount.

faq.q: What is a mortgage discharge fee?
faq.a: When you sell a property and pay off your home loan, lenders charge a discharge fee to remove the mortgage from the title. This is typically $300–$600.

faq.q: How is agent commission calculated?
faq.a: Agent commission is a percentage of the sale price, agreed before listing. It varies by state — Queensland is typically 2%–3.5%, NSW and Victoria 1.5%–2.5%. Always get written quotes from multiple agents and compare the total cost including marketing.

faq.q: How does this calculator work out my payoff date?
faq.a: It simulates your mortgage month by month — applying your repayment, deducting interest, and reducing principal — until the balance reaches zero. The number of months gives your payoff timeline.

faq.q: What happens if my repayment doesn't cover the interest?
faq.a: If your monthly repayment is less than or equal to the monthly interest charge, your loan will never be paid off — the balance grows over time. This is called negative amortisation. The calculator will flag this situation.

faq.q: How can I pay off my mortgage faster?
faq.a: Making extra repayments, switching to fortnightly payments, and making lump sum payments are the three most effective strategies. Even $200 extra per month on a $500,000 loan at 6.5% can save over 4 years and $80,000 in interest.

faq.q: Does the calculator account for rate changes?
faq.a: No — the calculator assumes your interest rate stays constant for the entire loan term. If rates rise, your payoff timeline will extend; if rates fall, it will shorten.

title: Extra Repayment Calculator

faq.q: How much does $500 extra per month save on a $500,000 mortgage?
faq.a: On a $500,000 loan at 6.5% over 30 years, paying an extra $500 per month saves approximately $170,000 in interest and cuts about 9 years off the loan term.

faq.q: Is there a limit to how much extra I can repay?
faq.a: On variable rate loans, you can typically make unlimited extra repayments. On fixed rate loans, most lenders cap extra repayments at $10,000–$20,000 per year during the fixed period. Check your loan terms.

faq.q: What is the effect of making extra repayments early in the loan?
faq.a: Extra repayments are most powerful early in a loan when the outstanding balance (and therefore the interest component) is highest. Every dollar of extra principal reduction saves interest compounding over the remaining years.

faq.q: Can I use an offset account instead of extra repayments?
faq.a: Yes. An offset account achieves the same interest-saving effect — money in the offset reduces the balance on which interest is calculated. Some borrowers prefer offset accounts for flexibility (the money is accessible). Check whether your loan has a free offset account.

title: Lump Sum Payment Calculator
description: Find out how much interest you save and how much sooner your mortgage ends when you make a one-off lump sum payment.
metaDescription: Calculate the interest saved and time cut from your mortgage by making a lump sum payment. See your estimated payoff date and total savings.

faq.q: When is the best time to make a lump sum mortgage payment?
faq.a: Earlier is always better. A lump sum payment made in year 1 saves significantly more interest than the same payment made in year 10, because there are more years of compounding for the interest to accumulate. However, any lump sum payment is beneficial at any point in the loan.

faq.q: Does a lump sum payment reduce my repayments or shorten my loan?
faq.a: It depends on your lender. Most lenders keep your repayment the same but your loan ends earlier. Some allow you to revert to a lower minimum repayment — but keeping repayments the same maximises your interest savings.

faq.q: What is the best use of a windfall — lump sum on the mortgage or investing?
faq.a: This depends on your mortgage rate vs expected investment return, and your risk tolerance. If your mortgage rate is 6.5%, you need investments to return more than 6.5% after tax to come out ahead. For most Australians paying down the mortgage is a guaranteed, risk-free return.

faq.q: Are there fees for making lump sum payments on a fixed rate loan?
faq.a: Fixed rate loans often include break costs or limit extra repayments during the fixed period. On variable rate loans, lump sum payments are generally unlimited and fee-free. Always check your loan contract.

title: Fortnightly Payment Calculator
description: Calculate how much interest and time you save by switching from monthly to fortnightly mortgage repayments.
metaDescription: See how switching to fortnightly mortgage repayments saves you years and tens of thousands in interest. Free Australian calculator with instant results.

faq.q: Why does paying fortnightly save money compared to monthly?
faq.a: When you pay half your monthly repayment every fortnight, you make 26 half-payments per year — equivalent to 13 full monthly payments. That extra month's payment every year chips away at your principal faster, reducing the interest that compounds on the outstanding balance.

faq.q: How much can I save by switching to fortnightly payments?
faq.a: On a typical $500,000 mortgage at 6.5% over 30 years, switching to fortnightly payments can save approximately $70,000 in interest and shorten the loan by around 4–5 years.

faq.q: Do lenders allow fortnightly repayments?
faq.a: Most Australian lenders allow weekly, fortnightly, or monthly repayments. Check your loan contract — some lenders split the monthly payment in two rather than offering a true fortnightly schedule. True fortnightly (26 payments/year) saves more than semi-monthly (24 payments/year).

faq.q: Is the fortnightly repayment amount exactly half the monthly payment?
faq.a: In this calculator, yes — the fortnightly payment is half the standard monthly repayment. This is the most common method used by lenders, and it's this structure that creates the equivalent of one extra monthly payment per year.

title: Split Loan Calculator
description: Compare a split home loan (part fixed, part variable) against all-fixed and all-variable options to find your best strategy.
metaDescription: Calculate split loan repayments, blended interest rate, and total interest cost. Compare fixed vs variable vs split loan in one Australian calculator.
