faq.q: What is a split home loan?
faq.a: A split home loan divides your mortgage into two portions — one on a fixed interest rate (rate certainty for a set period) and one on a variable rate (flexibility for extra repayments and offset accounts). The split can be any ratio, e.g. 50/50, 70/30.

faq.q: What are the benefits of splitting your home loan?
faq.a: You get the best of both worlds: certainty on the fixed portion (protection against rate rises) and flexibility on the variable portion (extra repayments, redraw, offset accounts). It's a hedging strategy for borrowers uncertain about interest rate direction.

faq.q: What is a blended interest rate?
faq.a: The blended rate is the weighted average of your fixed and variable rates, based on how much of your loan is in each portion. A 50/50 split at 6.0% fixed and 6.5% variable gives a blended rate of 6.25%.

faq.q: Can I make extra repayments on a split loan?
faq.a: Yes, but only on the variable portion (unless your lender allows limited extra repayments on fixed). Most lenders allow unlimited extra repayments on variable rate portions, often with a linked offset account.

title: Luxury Car Tax (LCT) Calculator
description: Calculate the Luxury Car Tax payable on a new vehicle purchase in Australia for the 2024-25 financial year, including the higher threshold for fuel-efficient vehicles.
metaDescription: Calculate Australian Luxury Car Tax (LCT) for 2024-25. Instant results for standard and fuel-efficient vehicle thresholds. ATO-based formula.

inputs.label: Car Price
inputs.help: Enter the drive-away price including or excluding GST (select below)

faq.q: What is Luxury Car Tax (LCT)?
faq.a: LCT is a federal tax applied to luxury vehicles sold in Australia above a set threshold. For 2024-25, the threshold is $76,950 for standard vehicles and $89,332 for fuel-efficient vehicles (electric and hybrids). LCT is charged at 33% on the amount above the threshold, calculated as: (GST-inclusive price − threshold) × 33 ÷ 133.

faq.q: Do electric vehicles attract LCT in Australia?
faq.a: Electric vehicles are subject to LCT, but at a higher threshold of $89,332 for 2024-25 (compared to $76,950 for standard vehicles). This means many EVs that would attract LCT under the standard threshold are exempt under the fuel-efficient threshold.

faq.q: Who pays LCT — the buyer or the dealer?
faq.a: Technically the dealer pays LCT to the ATO and passes the cost on to the buyer in the vehicle price. You'll see it as a line item on the contract or included in the total drive-away price.

faq.q: Can businesses claim back LCT?
faq.a: Businesses can only claim back LCT if the vehicle is used for a purpose that attracts a LCT credit — for example, a primary producer or tourism operator. For most businesses, LCT is a non-refundable cost.

title: Novated Lease Calculator
description: Calculate the tax savings and net fortnightly cost of a novated lease versus buying a car outright, using 2024-25 Australian tax brackets.
metaDescription: Calculate novated lease savings in Australia. See your tax benefit, net fortnightly cost, and comparison to buying outright. 2024-25 tax rates.

faq.q: What is a novated lease?
faq.a: A novated lease is a three-way agreement between you, your employer, and a finance company. Your employer deducts lease payments from your pre-tax salary, reducing your taxable income. The result is a lower income tax bill, which reduces the effective cost of running a car.

faq.q: Who is eligible for a novated lease?
faq.a: Any PAYG employee whose employer offers salary packaging can access a novated lease. It's most commonly used by employees in the public sector, healthcare, and large corporations. Self-employed individuals are not eligible.

faq.q: Does FBT (Fringe Benefits Tax) apply to novated leases?
faq.a: FBT can apply to novated leases, but many arrangements use the Employee Contribution Method (ECM) to reduce or eliminate FBT by having the employee contribute an after-tax amount equal to the FBT liability. Electric vehicles are FBT-exempt if under the LCT threshold.

faq.q: What happens at the end of a novated lease?
faq.a: At end of term, you can pay the residual value to own the car outright, re-lease the vehicle, or hand it back. The residual value is set by the ATO based on the lease term.

faq.q: Is a novated lease worth it?
faq.a: It depends on your marginal tax rate, the car price, and how much you drive. The higher your income, the more tax you save. Employees in the 37%+ tax bracket typically benefit most. Always compare total cost of ownership including residual, FBT, and running costs.

title: Caravan Loan Calculator

faq.q: What interest rate should I expect on a caravan loan in Australia?
faq.a: Caravan loan rates vary widely depending on the lender, loan type, and your credit profile. Secured caravan loans (the caravan is collateral) typically range from 7%–12% p.a. Unsecured personal loans for caravans are usually higher, at 10%–18%. Always compare the comparison rate, not just the advertised rate.

faq.q: Can I get a caravan loan without a deposit?
faq.a: Some lenders offer 100% finance for caravans, but you'll pay a higher interest rate and the lender will need to be satisfied the caravan holds sufficient value. A deposit of 10%–20% typically gives you access to better rates and terms.

faq.q: What is a balloon payment on a caravan loan?
faq.a: A balloon payment defers a lump sum (commonly 20–40% of the loan) to the end of the term. This reduces your monthly repayments during the loan but leaves a large payment due at the end. It suits buyers who expect a lump sum at loan maturity or plan to refinance.

faq.q: How long can I finance a caravan for?
faq.a: Most Australian lenders offer caravan loans from 1 to 10 years. Longer terms reduce monthly repayments but significantly increase total interest paid. A 10-year loan on a $60,000 caravan at 9.5% costs about 60% more in interest than a 5-year loan.

title: Commercial Hire Purchase Calculator

faq.q: What is a Commercial Hire Purchase (CHP)?
faq.a: A CHP is a business finance arrangement where the lender purchases the asset and hires it to you for a fixed term. Ownership transfers to you at the end of the term once all payments (including any balloon) are made. Unlike a lease, you can claim depreciation and interest as business deductions.

faq.q: Can I claim GST on a Commercial Hire Purchase?
faq.a: If your business is GST registered, you can claim the full GST input tax credit on the asset purchase price in your next BAS, rather than spreading it across repayments. This is a significant cash flow advantage of CHP over some other finance structures.

faq.q: What are the tax benefits of CHP for businesses?
faq.a: Interest payments are tax deductible, and the asset is depreciable under Division 40. Under the ATO's instant asset write-off (where eligible), you may be able to deduct the full asset cost in the year of purchase. Consult your accountant for your specific entitlements.

faq.q: What is a balloon payment and should I use one?
faq.a: A balloon payment defers a percentage of the loan to the end of the term, reducing regular repayments. It suits businesses that want to free up cash flow during the loan and plan to refinance, sell the asset, or pay from business profits at maturity.

title: Balance Transfer Calculator

faq.q: What is a balance transfer?
faq.a: A balance transfer moves your existing credit card debt to a new card — often at 0% or a very low interest rate for a promotional period (typically 6–24 months). This gives you time to pay down the balance without accumulating interest at the usual 20%+ rate.

faq.q: What is the balance transfer fee?
faq.a: Most balance transfer offers charge a fee of 1%–3% of the amount you're transferring. For example, a 2% fee on an $8,000 balance costs $160. This fee is added to your new card balance and should be factored into your savings calculation.

faq.q: What happens after the promotional period ends?
faq.a: Once the promo period ends, any remaining balance reverts to the card's standard purchase rate — typically 19%–22% p.a. This is the revert rate. If you haven't paid off the balance by then, you'll start accruing interest at the full rate.

faq.q: Is a balance transfer always worth it?
faq.a: Not always. The transfer fee can outweigh the interest savings if the promo period is short or the balance is small. This calculator shows you the net saving (or cost) of transferring so you can decide with real numbers.

faq.q: Can I spend on a balance transfer card?
faq.a: Be careful — new purchases on a balance transfer card often attract the full purchase rate from day one, not the promo rate. It's best to avoid using a BT card for new spending and focus on paying down the transferred balance during the promo period.

title: Personal Loan Repayment Calculator
description: Calculate personal loan repayments weekly, fortnightly or monthly, total interest paid, and a full amortisation schedule for any Australian personal loan.
metaDescription: Calculate personal loan repayments by week, fortnight or month. Get total interest, total cost, and a full amortisation schedule. Free Australian calculator.

faq.q: What is the average interest rate for a personal loan in Australia?
faq.a: Personal loan rates in Australia typically range from 6.99% to 25% p.a. depending on the lender, your credit profile, and whether the loan is secured. The comparison rate is the most accurate measure of total cost — it includes fees as well as the interest rate.

faq.q: What is the difference between a secured and unsecured personal loan?
faq.a: A secured loan uses an asset (such as a car) as collateral. This reduces the lender's risk, so rates are lower — typically 6%–12%. An unsecured personal loan has no collateral requirement but carries higher rates (10%–25%) and is approved based on your creditworthiness alone.

faq.q: Does the repayment frequency affect how much I pay?
faq.a: Weekly or fortnightly repayments can save a small amount compared to monthly, as you're paying more frequently and interest accrues on a lower balance. For personal loans over short terms, the difference is modest — typically a few hundred dollars.

faq.q: What is an amortisation schedule?
faq.a: An amortisation schedule shows how each payment is split between interest and principal over the life of the loan. Early payments are mostly interest; later payments are mostly principal. This calculator shows the first and last 12 payments for any term.

title: Comparison Rate Calculator
description: Calculate the true comparison rate for any Australian loan by factoring in fees — both for your actual loan scenario and the regulatory $150,000/25-year standard.
metaDescription: Calculate the true comparison rate for any Australian home or personal loan including all fees. Understand how fees affect your real borrowing cost.

faq.q: What is a comparison rate?
faq.a: A comparison rate is an interest rate that includes most fees and charges in a single figure, making it easier to compare the true cost of different loans. Australian law requires lenders to display a comparison rate alongside their advertised rate, calculated on a standard $150,000 loan over 25 years.

faq.q: Why does the regulatory comparison rate use $150,000 over 25 years?
faq.a: This standardised loan size and term is set by Australian law (National Consumer Credit Protection Act) so that consumers can compare like-for-like. However, because fees are fixed amounts, a smaller loan inflates the impact of fees — making the regulatory comparison rate misleading for large mortgages. This calculator shows both the regulatory rate and your actual scenario rate.

faq.q: What fees are included in a comparison rate?
faq.a: Comparison rates include establishment fees, monthly fees, and annual fees. They exclude government charges (stamp duty, title insurance), redraw fees, and fees conditional on an event (like break costs). This is why two identical loans can have different comparison rates.

faq.q: How much do fees add to my interest rate?
faq.a: A $600 establishment fee on a $500,000 mortgage over 30 years adds less than 0.05% to the effective rate — minimal. But a $600 establishment fee on a $50,000 personal loan over 3 years can add 0.5%–0.8% to the effective rate. Fees hurt more on small, short loans.

title: Net Worth Calculator
description: Calculate your personal net worth by entering your assets and liabilities, and compare your result to Australian median benchmarks for your age group.
metaDescription: Calculate your net worth in Australia and see how you compare to the median for your age group. Uses ABS Household Income and Wealth data (2021-22).
keywords: australian median net worth by age

inputs.label: Your Age
inputs.help: Used to compare your net worth against ABS benchmarks for your age group

faq.q: What is net worth?
faq.a: Net worth is the total value of everything you own (assets) minus everything you owe (liabilities). It's the single most important measure of financial health. A positive net worth means your assets exceed your debts; a negative net worth (more common for young Australians) means liabilities exceed assets.

faq.q: Should I include superannuation in my net worth?
faq.a: Yes. The ABS includes superannuation in its net worth calculations, and this calculator follows that convention. Super is a real financial asset — it's just locked until preservation age. Including it gives you the most accurate picture of your wealth.

faq.q: What is the median net worth for Australians?
faq.a: According to ABS data (2021-22), median net worth varies by age: under 25 (~$35,000), 25-34 (~$150,000), 35-44 (~$500,000), 45-54 (~$850,000), 55-64 (~$1,100,000), and 65+ (~$1,000,000). These figures include superannuation.

faq.q: How can I improve my net worth?
faq.a: The two levers are growing assets (saving more, investing in growth assets like shares and property) and reducing liabilities (paying down high-interest debt first). Over time, compounding returns on investments and the paydown of mortgages naturally increase net worth for most Australians.

faq.q: How much life insurance do I need in Australia?
faq.a: A common rule of thumb is 10 times your annual income, but a needs-based calculation is more accurate. You should consider income replacement for your dependants, clearing all debts (mortgage and other loans), funding children's education (~$50,000 per child), and covering funeral and estate costs (~$12,500). Subtract any existing cover you already have.

faq.q: Does superannuation include life insurance?
faq.a: Most Australian superannuation funds include default death and total permanent disability (TPD) cover for eligible members. The amount is often modest — typically 2–4 units of cover worth $50,000–$400,000. Check your super statement or fund website to find your default cover amount.

faq.q: When should I review my life insurance needs?
faq.a: Review your cover whenever you have a major life event: buying a home, having a child, salary increases, or paying down significant debt. Life insurance needs generally decrease as you age — as debts are paid down and children become financially independent.

faq.q: What is the difference between life insurance and income protection?
faq.a: Life insurance pays a lump sum to your beneficiaries upon your death. Income protection insurance pays a monthly benefit (typically 70%–85% of your income) if you are unable to work due to illness or injury. Both serve different purposes and are often recommended together.

title: Income Protection Calculator
description: Estimate your income protection insurance benefit, indicative premium, and the value of cover based on your income, occupation, age, and policy settings.
metaDescription: Calculate income protection insurance benefit and estimated premium in Australia. See how occupation, waiting period, and benefit period affect your cover and cost.
keywords: how much income protection do i need

faq.q: What is income protection insurance?
faq.a: Income protection insurance pays a monthly benefit — typically 75%–85% of your pre-disability income — if you are unable to work due to illness or injury. Payments continue from the end of the waiting period until you recover, the benefit period ends, or you reach age 65 (for to-65 policies).

faq.q: Is income protection insurance tax deductible in Australia?
faq.a: Yes. Premiums for income protection insurance held outside superannuation are generally fully tax-deductible in Australia. Policies held inside super are paid with pre-tax super contributions, but benefits paid out may be taxable at your marginal rate. Consult your tax adviser.

faq.q: What is the waiting period?
faq.a: The waiting period (also called the excess period) is the time you must be unable to work before payments begin. Common options are 14, 30, 60, or 90 days. A longer waiting period lowers your premium — choose a waiting period that matches your sick leave entitlements or emergency fund runway.

faq.q: What is the benefit period?
faq.a: The benefit period is the maximum length of time the insurer will pay your monthly benefit. Options include 2 years, 5 years, or to age 65. Longer benefit periods cost more but provide significantly better long-term protection. A 2-year benefit period is the most common for shorter-term cover.

faq.q: Do occupation categories affect income protection premiums?
faq.a: Yes, significantly. Insurers classify occupations by risk. Professionals in office-based roles attract the lowest premiums. Heavy manual workers (tradespeople, labourers) can pay 2x–3x more for equivalent cover due to higher injury risk. Your occupation class is one of the biggest drivers of premium.

faq.q: What are macros?
faq.a: Macros (macronutrients) are the three main nutrients your body needs in large quantities: protein, carbohydrates, and fat. Each provides energy (calories) and plays different roles in your body. Protein builds and repairs muscle, carbs fuel activity, and fat supports hormones and cell health.

faq.q: How much protein do I need per day?
faq.a: For most active Australians, 1.6-2.2g of protein per kilogram of body weight is recommended. This calculator uses 2g/kg as a solid baseline for anyone exercising regularly. If you're sedentary, 1.2-1.6g/kg may be sufficient.

faq.q: What is TDEE?
faq.a: TDEE (Total Daily Energy Expenditure) is the total number of calories your body burns in a day, including your basal metabolic rate plus calories burned through activity. It's the starting point for calculating how much you should eat to lose, maintain, or gain weight.

faq.q: Should I track macros or just calories?
faq.a: Tracking macros gives you more control over body composition. Two people eating the same calories can get very different results depending on their macro split. If your goal is to build muscle or lose fat while preserving muscle, tracking macros is significantly more effective than calories alone.

faq.q: How accurate is this macro calculator?
faq.a: This calculator uses the Mifflin-St Jeor equation, which research shows is the most accurate BMR formula for most people. However, individual metabolism varies. Use these numbers as a starting point and adjust based on your actual results over 2-3 weeks.

faq.q: What are the five food groups in Australia?
faq.a: The Australian Dietary Guidelines identify five core food groups: vegetables and legumes, fruit, grain (cereal) foods, lean meats and protein alternatives, and milk/yoghurt/cheese (dairy). Each group provides different essential nutrients, and eating the recommended serves from all five groups supports overall health.

faq.q: What counts as one serve of vegetables?
faq.a: One standard serve of vegetables is about 75g, which equals roughly ½ cup of cooked vegetables, 1 cup of raw salad, or ½ medium potato. Most Australians don't eat enough vegetables — the average adult needs 5-6 serves per day.

faq.q: Why do pregnant women need more grains?
faq.a: Pregnant women need 8.5 serves of grain foods per day (compared to 6 for non-pregnant women) because grains provide essential B vitamins, iron, and energy needed to support the growing baby. Wholegrain and high-fibre options are recommended for at least two-thirds of grain serves.

faq.q: Do these guidelines apply to children under 2?
faq.a: No. The Australian Dietary Guidelines provide specific food group serves for ages 2 and above. Children under 2 have different nutritional needs, including continued breastfeeding or formula. Speak with your paediatrician or child health nurse for age-appropriate feeding advice.

faq.q: Are these guidelines from the Australian Government?
faq.a: Yes. This calculator is based on the Australian Dietary Guidelines published by the National Health and Medical Research Council (NHMRC) through the Eat for Health program. These are the official dietary recommendations for Australians.

faq.q: How much does a home-cooked meal cost in Australia?
faq.a: The average home-cooked dinner in Australia costs $10-$15 for a family of four using standard supermarket ingredients. This can drop to $6-$8 per meal with budget planning, or rise to $20+ with premium ingredients. The key variable is whether you shop at Aldi, Coles, or Woolworths, and whether you meal plan.

faq.q: Are meal kits cheaper than cooking from scratch?
faq.a: Generally no. Meal kits like HelloFresh cost around $8-$12 per person per serve in Australia, which is more expensive than cooking from scratch for most families. However, meal kits reduce food waste and save planning time. For singles or couples, the cost difference is smaller and meal kits can actually save money compared to buying ingredients that go to waste.

faq.q: How much does the average Australian family spend on takeaway?
faq.a: Australian families spend an average of $60-$100 per week on takeaway and food delivery. With delivery app fees and surcharges, the average Uber Eats order for a family of four is around $70-$90. Cutting just one takeaway meal per week can save $3,000-$5,000 per year.

faq.q: What is the cheapest way to feed a family in Australia?
faq.a: Batch cooking and meal planning with supermarket specials is the most cost-effective approach. Shop at Aldi for staples, buy seasonal produce, cook in bulk on weekends, and use a freezer. A family of four can eat well for $120-$150 per week with disciplined planning.

faq.q: Does this calculator include lunch and breakfast?
faq.a: This calculator focuses on dinner meals as that's where the biggest cost variation occurs between home cooking, meal kits, and takeaway. You can adjust the "meals per week" number to include other meals if you want a more complete picture.

keywords: how much to spend on groceries australia

faq.q: How much should a family of 4 spend on groceries in Australia?
faq.a: A family of four in metro Australia typically spends $250-$400 per week on groceries in 2025-26, depending on budget style. Budget-conscious families can manage around $220/week by shopping at Aldi, using specials, and meal planning. Comfortable spending sits around $360-$400/week with premium brands and organic produce.

faq.q: Why are groceries more expensive in regional and remote areas?
faq.a: Transport costs, limited competition, and supply chain logistics push grocery prices higher outside capital cities. Regional areas typically pay 5-10% more, while remote communities can pay 20-30% more for basic groceries. This is recognised by the Australian Government through remote area allowances.

faq.q: Is a vegetarian diet cheaper than eating meat?
faq.a: Generally yes. Plant-based proteins like lentils, chickpeas, and tofu are significantly cheaper per kilogram than meat in Australian supermarkets. A vegetarian diet typically costs 10-15% less than a standard diet. Vegan diets can save even more, though specialty vegan products (like plant-based cheese) can offset some savings.

faq.q: Why does a gluten-free diet cost more?
faq.a: Gluten-free specialty products (bread, pasta, flour, snacks) typically cost 2-3 times more than their standard equivalents in Australian supermarkets. While naturally gluten-free foods (rice, potatoes, meat, vegetables) are the same price, the premium on processed gluten-free products adds roughly 15-20% to a typical grocery bill.

faq.q: How can I reduce my grocery spending?
faq.a: The biggest savings come from meal planning, shopping at Aldi for staples, buying seasonal produce, cooking from scratch, and reducing food waste. Using apps like Frugl to compare prices and shopping supermarket specials can save 20-30%. Batch cooking on weekends also reduces the temptation to order takeaway.
